summary: Operation Epic Fury launched Feb 28, 2026. Pentagon confirmed $11.3B in first 6 days (primarily munitions replacement — $3.1B in first 100 hours alone per CSIS). CSIS estimated $16.5B by Day 12. Sustained rate settled at ~$500M/day by Days 25–32. Total through Day 32 (Apr 1): ~$27.5B. On March 19, the Pentagon submitted a $200B supplemental budget request covering projected full-campaign costs. Penn Wharton Budget Model projected costs could reach $47B if operations continued through end of April. CEASEFIRE: The U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire on April 8, 2026 (Day 40), mediated by Pakistan and brokered via the Islamabad Accords. The White House declared core military objectives achieved in 38 days — more than 85% of Iran's defense industrial base, including the majority of its ballistic missiles and long-range drones, destroyed. Iran claimed victory, asserting it forced the U.S. to accept its 10-point plan (including lifting sanctions and U.S. withdrawal from regional bases); Trump called the 10-point plan a 'workable basis on which to negotiate.' The Strait of Hormuz was reopened under the ceasefire. Israel did NOT join the ceasefire — Netanyahu declared Lebanon operations continue separately. The cost counter is frozen at the ceasefire date (Day 40, ~$31B total). CEASEFIRE FRAGILITY (Apr 9-10, 2026): By April 9, Iran accused the U.S. of violating the ceasefire on three counts: continued Israeli attacks on Lebanon, a drone entering Iranian airspace, and U.S. refusal to grant uranium enrichment rights. The Strait of Hormuz was reported blocked again on April 9. US and Iranian negotiators met in Islamabad on April 10 (Day 42) to begin comprehensive talks under Pakistani mediation. The ceasefire's durability remains uncertain. Note: concurrent 2026 Israel–Hezbollah/Lebanon operations tracked separately under 'lebanon2026'. ISLAMABAD TALKS COLLAPSE (Apr 12, 2026): U.S.-Iran peace talks in Islamabad ended without agreement after 21 hours of negotiations. VP Vance departed after Iran refused to accept Washington’s terms; Iran FM Ghalibaf publicly blamed the U.S. for the failure. Iran’s Foreign Ministry spokesman cited ‘significant gaps’ on several major issues. Core U.S. demand — an affirmative commitment from Iran not to seek nuclear weapons — was rejected. Iran’s demands included: lifting of sanctions, guaranteed uranium enrichment rights, right to charge ships through the Strait of Hormuz, and an end to Israeli attacks on Lebanon. The failure leaves the fragile two-week ceasefire (announced April 8, due to expire ~April 22) in serious question. The Strait of Hormuz remains contested and the Hormuz clause of the ceasefire has not been verifiably implemented. US MILITARY BLOCKADE (Apr 13-14, 2026): After the Islamabad talks collapsed on April 12 and Trump announced he 'doesn't care if Iran returns to talks,' the U.S. military began a formal blockade of Iranian ports effective 10 a.m. ET on April 14, 2026 (Day 46). U.S. Central Command confirmed only ships entering/leaving Iranian ports would be blockaded; freedom of navigation for other vessels was declared intact. Trump warned any Iranian ships challenging the blockade would be 'eliminated.' The IRGC vowed to retaliate. The UK (PM Starmer) said it would not join the blockade. A coalition of 40+ nations (UK-led, NATO-affiliated) began planning to keep Hormuz open. Brent crude rose to near $100/barrel — up ~40% since the war began. The ceasefire technically remains in effect but is due to expire ~April 21. A Harvard academic (CNBC, Apr 14) estimated total U.S. costs could reach $1 trillion if the conflict resumes. Pakistani, Egyptian, and Turkish mediators were continuing back-channel contacts; Trump hinted 'something could be happening' in the next two days. The blockade represents a dramatic escalation despite the formal ceasefire. BLOCKADE FULLY IMPLEMENTED (Apr 15-16, 2026): CENTCOM confirmed the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports is now "completely" cutting off Iran's international sea trade — which powers ~90% of its economy. Gen. Dan Caine (Chairman of the Joint Chiefs) clarified the blockade applies to ships heading into or from Iranian ports in territorial seas and international waters. The blockade is estimated to cost Iran approximately $435 million a day in combined economic damage. A second round of U.S.-Iran talks remains under discussion; JD Vance is expected to lead should a meeting occur before the ceasefire expires. Trump told the New York Post that talks "could be happening over next two days" in Islamabad. The ceasefire is set to expire approximately April 21. Iran's FM Araghchi accused the U.S. of "maximalism, shifting goalposts, and blockade." Iran has been removing debris from underground missile bunkers during the ceasefire (CNN satellite imagery, Apr 16). An Iranian Navy frigate (IRIS Dena) was sunk by USS Charlotte in the Indian Ocean. IMF cut 2026 global growth forecast to 3.1% citing Hormuz disruptions. Brent crude near $100/barrel. The ceasefire counter remains frozen at April 8 (Day 40). APRIL 17, 2026 UPDATE: Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announced the Strait of Hormuz was "completely open" to all shipping; oil prices dropped ~11% on the announcement. Trump responded on Truth Social that the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports would remain "in full force ... until such time as our transaction with Iran is 100% complete." Iranian Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf said Iran would close Hormuz again if the U.S. continued its blockade. Iranian sources reported the next round of U.S.-Iran talks would take place in Pakistan on Monday, April 20. The April 8 ceasefire (Day 40) remains technically in effect but is due to expire April 22. Separately, Trump announced a 10-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire that took effect April 17. ESCALATION (Apr 18-21, 2026 — DAYS 50-53): The fragile two-week ceasefire is collapsing as it nears the April 22 expiration date. On April 18 (Day 50), Iran reversed its April 17 decision to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, reinstating restrictions until the U.S. fully lifts its port blockade — Iran said the blockade costs it ~$400M/day in lost revenue and threatens permanent damage to oil wells if not ended by April 26. On April 19 (Day 51), the USS Spruance disabled and seized the Iranian-flagged cargo vessel M/V Touska in the Gulf of Oman after a six-hour standoff in which the ship's crew refused repeated warnings to comply with the U.S. naval blockade. On April 20 (Day 52), CENTCOM confirmed U.S. forces have directed 27 vessels to turn around or return to Iranian ports since the blockade began. President Trump publicly stated that extending the ceasefire is 'highly unlikely' and wrote on Truth Social: 'We're offering a very fair and reasonable deal, and I hope they take it because, if they don't, the United States is going to knock out every single power plant, and every single bridge, in Iran.' Only 16 ships traversed the Strait of Hormuz on Monday April 20 — among the quietest days since the conflict began. No tankers crossed on April 20 at all, per tracking data. VP Vance and top U.S. officials are expected to depart for Islamabad for a potential second round of talks, but Iran's attendance remains uncertain. The April 8 ceasefire is scheduled to expire April 22, 2026.
comparables: The total has already exceeded the Manhattan Project in today's dollars — reached in under 5 weeks of fighting
At $500M/day, this operation spends the WHO's entire annual global health budget ($6.8B) every 14 days
Each month of operations ($15B) could fully fund the World Food Programme for nearly 2 years

casualties.note: Figures remain contested at ceasefire. Iran has not published a verified military vs. civilian breakdown. Al Jazeera's live tracker on Day 34 recorded 2,076 total killed and 26,500+ wounded — this reflects all causes and is likely an undercount. Iran International (Mar 31) cited 4,700 IRGC/security forces KIA separately; Hengaw Organization put Iranian military/security killed at 6,620 by April 8 — far higher than official/media totals, suggesting significant military casualties withheld. The Intercept (Apr 1) alleged CENTCOM systematically undercounted U.S. casualties. DCAS shows 365 confirmed U.S. troops wounded, 13 KIA. The ceasefire took effect April 8 (Day 40) but was immediately complicated by continued Israeli strikes on Lebanon. APR 20, 2026 UPDATE: CENTCOM's confirmed U.S. military figures (13 KIA, 381 wounded through April 8) remain unchanged through the ceasefire and blockade enforcement periods. IranWarLive OSINT has tracked 4,500+ verified Iranian casualties across all regional fronts. The M/V Touska seizure (Apr 19) did not result in reported casualties. Total Iranian military/security KIA estimates remain contested — Hengaw's 6,620 figure (Apr 8) is the highest independent estimate; Iran International's 4,700 IRGC/security KIA is the median.

summary: Hezbollah resumed rocket fire against northern Israel on March 2, 2026 — breaking the Nov 2024 ceasefire — in response to Op. Epic Fury against Iran. Israel launched an expanded air campaign over Lebanon, followed by ground operations in southern Lebanon beginning March 16. By late March 2026 Israel's combined war costs (Iran + Lebanon) were running at ~$1.6B/week. $150M/day reflects Israel-specific Lebanon operational costs; anchor of $2B captures the initial bombardment phase. CRITICAL ESCALATION: On April 8, 2026 — the same day the U.S.-Iran ceasefire was announced — Israel launched 'Operation Eternal Darkness,' its largest single-day strikes of the entire 2026 Lebanon War: 50+ fighter jets, 160 munitions, 100+ air strikes across Lebanon including central Beirut without warning. Lebanon's Civil Defence reported 254 killed and 1,165 wounded in that single day (some sources say 300+). Netanyahu explicitly stated the Iran ceasefire 'does not include Lebanon.' Hezbollah halted attacks on Israel temporarily after the Iran ceasefire, but Israel continued operations. Cumulative killed in Lebanon through April 9, 2026: ~2,100+ (Civil Defence estimate; Lebanese health ministry logged 1,739 separately — methodology diverges); wounded: ~7,100+; displaced: 1.2M+. APRIL 9–12 UPDATE: On April 9 surprise ceasefire talks between Israel and Lebanon emerged, with Israel indicating readiness to discuss a framework; however, Israel simultaneously launched further strikes. On April 7, Israel deployed the 98th Division into southern Lebanon. On April 11, Israeli strikes killed at least 18 more people across southern Lebanon. Israel approved direct ambassador-level talks with Lebanon at the U.S. State Department for the week of April 14 while simultaneously rejecting any ceasefire with Hezbollah before or during those talks. Hezbollah rejected talks absent a prior ceasefire and Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon. As of April 12, the war continues with no ceasefire; Washington talks are scheduled for the week ahead. Concurrent U.S. and Israeli costs for the Iran campaign are tracked under Op. Epic Fury. APRIL 13-14 UPDATE: Lebanese Ministry of Health / Civil Defence reported a cumulative death toll of 2,055 killed and 6,588 wounded as of April 13, 2026 (CGTN). On April 13, Israeli strikes killed at least 6 more people in southern Lebanon, including strikes on Bint Jbeil and surrounding areas where IDF ground troops are conducting operations. IDF claims to have killed over 100 Hezbollah fighters in the Bint Jbeil area. Ambassador-level talks between Israel and Lebanon are scheduled to begin at the U.S. State Department on April 15. Lebanon's culture minister described the goal as securing 'a pause in military activity, if not a ceasefire.' War total: 2,055+ killed, 6,588+ wounded; 1M+ displaced. PEACE TALKS BEGIN (Apr 14-16, 2026): Israel and Lebanon held their first direct negotiations since 1983, hosted at the U.S. State Department in Washington on April 14, 2026. Secretary of State Marco Rubio chaired the meeting; Israeli, Lebanese, and U.S. ambassadors and UN Ambassador Mike Waltz participated. The session was described as preliminary — focused on logistics rather than substance. Both sides said formal negotiations will proceed; no date set, no ceasefire agreed. Rubio said: "This is a process, not an event." Israel demands Lebanon disarm all non-state armed groups including Hezbollah; Lebanon demands full implementation of the 2024 agreement and Israeli withdrawal from its territory. Israel has reportedly proposed dividing southern Lebanon into three security zones. Trump announced on April 16 that further talks would begin April 17. HEZBOLLAH REJECTION: Hezbollah Secretary-General Naim Qassem called the talks "futile" and "a free concession," saying Hezbollah would not be bound by outcomes and would not disarm. Hezbollah fired rockets at IDF soldiers in southern Lebanon on April 15, wounding five (one seriously). War continues with no ceasefire. CUMULATIVE TOLL (Apr 15, 2026): Lebanese MoH reports 2,164 killed and 7,061 wounded. CEASEFIRE (Apr 17, 2026): President Trump announced on April 16 that Israel and Lebanon had agreed to a 10-day truce, which formally went into effect April 17, 2026 — ending 46 days of active combat. Trump invited PM Netanyahu and President Aoun to the White House for the first bilateral talks of their tenures. Core disagreement remains: Israel demands Hezbollah disarmament; Lebanon demands Israeli withdrawal from its territory and full 2024 agreement implementation. Hezbollah publicly rejected the ceasefire framework. The counter is frozen at April 17, 2026 (Day 46). Final cumulative toll (Lebanese MoH/Civil Defence): ~2,200–2,300 killed; ~7,100–7,400+ wounded; ~1.2M displaced. Peace negotiations continue in Washington; ceasefire durability remains contingent on Hezbollah's response and Israeli follow-through. APRIL 18-21, 2026 UPDATE: The 10-day ceasefire has held since April 17. Lebanese Ministry of Health cumulative toll (March 2 – April 16, 2026): 2,294 killed, 7,544 wounded. Israel killed at least 2,196 in its war on Lebanon (Wikipedia aggregate). Over 1 million displaced during the war. Displaced Lebanese have begun returning to devastated towns in southern Lebanon — many homes destroyed. Ambassador-level Washington talks continue in parallel; no permanent ceasefire or framework agreement yet. UN experts condemned Israel's 'unprecedented bombing' in Lebanon in the days preceding the ceasefire. Ceasefire durability is contingent on Hezbollah non-action and Israeli follow-through; no extension announced as of April 21.
comparables: The $150M/day rate exceeds the UN peacekeeping force in Lebanon's (UNIFIL) entire annual budget ($490M) — every 3 days
One month of this conflict ($4.5B) surpasses all international reconstruction aid delivered to Lebanon since the 2020 Beirut port explosion
At this rate, a year of fighting would consume Lebanon's entire pre-war GDP ($18B) nearly three times over

(Civil Defence cumulative estimate); Lebanese Ministry of Health: ~2,020+ killed, 6,436+ wounded (as of April 11, 2026, incorporating April 8 Operation Eternal Darkness count and subsequent strikes including 18 killed April 11). Approximately 1.2 million people (~20% of Lebanon's population) have been displaced. IDF claims 700+ Hezbollah operatives killed; Hezbollah sources put losses at ~400 fighters. Hezbollah halted attacks on Israel after the Iran ceasefire was announced, but Israel explicitly excluded Lebanon from the ceasefire and launched its largest strikes of the war the same day. Iran demanded any ceasefire cover Lebanon — that condition was not met. APRIL 10-11 UPDATE: Israel approved direct ambassador-level talks with Lebanon at the U.S. State Department for the week of April 14 while simultaneously rejecting any ceasefire with Hezbollah before or during those talks. Hezbollah rejected talks absent a prior ceasefire and Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon. APRIL 13, 2026: Lebanese Ministry of Health confirmed 2,055 killed / 6,588 wounded cumulative. APRIL 15, 2026 UPDATE: Lebanese MoH / Qatar News Agency reported cumulative 2,164 killed and 7,061 wounded as of April 15, 2026. Five IDF soldiers wounded in Hezbollah rocket attack on April 15. First direct Israel-Lebanon talks in four decades began April 14 at U.S. State Department (mediated by Secretary Rubio). Hezbollah rejected the talks and vowed to continue fighting. Ambassador-level talks are continuing as of April 16 with Trump announcing further sessions scheduled for April 17. APRIL 16-21, 2026 UPDATE: Lebanese MoH released cumulative toll: 2,294 killed, 7,544 wounded for the full March 2 – April 16, 2026 war period. The 10-day cessation of hostilities began 17:00 EST April 16 and has held through April 21. Over 1 million people displaced during the war — many now returning to destroyed towns. No casualties reported from the ceasefire period (Apr 17-21) per available sources.

summary: Combined: Russia ~$165B/yr (SIPRI/UkraineWorld 2026 budget: 14.9T roubles, 6.3% of GDP) + Ukraine ~$71B/yr (2025 defense budget) + European aid ~$65B/yr = ~$301B/yr or ~$825M/day. Total Western aid committed: $188B+ (CFR, Dec 2025). US withdrew 99% of direct support in 2025; Europe increased contribution but has not fully compensated. Russia's military spending as % of GDP peaked at 7.5% in 2025, declining slightly to 6.3% in 2026 (14.9T roubles planned) — though budget amendments expected as higher oil prices from the Iran war ease fiscal pressure (SIPRI, 2026). As of April 2026, Russian forces occupy roughly 20% of Ukraine. During March 10–April 7, Russian forces gained 17 sq miles — slow incremental gains continuing. MARCH 2026 LOSSES: Russia suffered its highest single-month casualties since 2022 in March 2026 — 35,351 killed or wounded in one month (United24 Media / Ukrainian General Staff). EASTER CEASEFIRE: Putin announced a temporary 30-hour Orthodox Easter ceasefire from April 11 (16:00 Moscow time) to end of April 12, 2026; Zelensky agreed, stating Ukraine was 'ready for symmetrical steps.' Historical precedent from 2025's Easter ceasefire showed both sides accused the other of violations — durability uncertain. Broader peace talks in Abu Dhabi and Geneva in early 2026 have stalled over territorial impasse. EASTER CEASEFIRE FAILS (Apr 11-12, 2026): Putin ordered a 32-hour ceasefire beginning 16:00 Moscow time April 11 through end of April 12 for Orthodox Easter; Zelensky agreed. Hours before it took effect, Russia struck Ukraine with 160 drones (133 intercepted/shot down), killing 2 in Odessa. The ceasefire largely failed: by 7:00 a.m. April 12, Ukraine reported 2,299 Russian violations (28 assault actions, 479 shellings, 747 attack drone strikes, 1,045 FPV strikes). Russia’s Defense Ministry countered that Ukraine committed 1,971 violations (258 artillery/tank shots, 1,329 FPV strikes, 375 munition drops). The Easter ceasefire expired at end of day April 12. The broader ceasefire remains in effect in name only; both sides are trading accusations. Russian advances have slowed since late 2025, with fighting near a standstill on most of the front. APRIL 14, 2026 UPDATE: Ukrainian General Staff reported total Russian personnel casualties at 1,312,960 as of April 14, 2026 (empr.media). BBC/Mediazona open-source investigation identified nearly 209,000 confirmed Russian KIA by name as of April 10, 2026 — up from 207,552+ on April 3. 125 combat engagements recorded on April 14; Russia carried out 1 missile strike, 70 airstrikes, 246 guided aerial bombs, 10,256 kamikaze drones, and 3,828 shellings. Frontline remains largely static with slow incremental Russian gains. APRIL 15-16, 2026 UPDATE: Russia launched a mass overnight strike on Ukraine on April 16, hitting Kyiv, Dnipro, and Odesa — residential buildings destroyed, at least 1 killed and 6 injured in Odessa alone. Ukraine struck back, hitting the Tuapse Oil Refinery (Rosneft, Krasnodar Krai). 212 combat engagements on April 15; Russia deployed 6,672 kamikaze drones and 2,917 shellings. Ukraine intercepted 20/21 missiles and 349/361 drones. Germany announced €300M for Ukrainian long-range drones (Boris Pistorius, Apr 15). Ukrainian General Staff reported Russian total casualties at 1,315,070 as of April 16 (+1,100 in 24hrs) — empr.media. The Easter ceasefire failure (April 11-12) confirmed that neither side has the strategic appetite for a durable halt; Russia immediately resumed full operations. Russia gained control of Vovchanski Khutory (Dnipropetrovsk region), advancing toward Bochkove. APRIL 16-17, 2026 MASS ATTACK: Russia launched its largest aerial assault of 2026 overnight April 16 — 19 ballistic missiles, 25 cruise missiles, and 659 drones targeting Kyiv, Odesa, Dnipro, and Zaporizhzhia. At least 18 killed (including a 12-year-old in Kyiv) and 118 wounded: 4+ killed and 50+ injured in Kyiv; 9 killed and 23 injured in Odesa; 3 killed and 36+ injured in Dnipro; 1 killed in Zaporizhzhia. Ukraine intercepted 8/19 ballistic missiles, 23/25 cruise missiles, and 636/659 drones. A drone crashed into an 18-story residential building in western Kyiv. Ukrainian officials called it "the deadliest aerial assault of the year." Analysts noted the Iran war has drained Ukraine's air defense munitions. The Easter ceasefire failure followed by this mass attack underscores that the broader ceasefire exists in name only. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: Russia suffered its highest monthly casualties since the February 2022 invasion — Ukraine's General Staff tallied 35,351 Russian casualties in March 2026 (29% above February), with drones causing 96% of them. Ukraine General Staff reports cumulative Russian losses of 1,311,180 troops as of April 12, 2026; equipment losses as of April 20: 11,884 tanks, 24,422 armored fighting vehicles, 40,396 artillery systems. Russia suffered 316 casualties per square kilometer captured in Q1 2026 — versus 120 per sq km in 2025. Ukraine's defense ministry states Russia has been unable to replace all losses since December 2025. In the week of April 17, Russia lost ~1,000 soldiers per day (KIA/WIA). IMF estimates Ukraine spends €172M/day (~$1.2B/week) on the war. EU has ratified a $90B multiyear loan for 2026. Total European military aid to Ukraine exceeds €72B by April 2026 (Kiel Institute). Ukraine approved a record 2026 wartime budget of ₴4.8T ($113.8B). APRIL 20-21, 2026 UPDATE: Ukrainian General Staff reported cumulative Russian personnel casualties at ~1,319,270 as of April 20 (+~1,050 in the prior 24 hours). NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte stated the Russian army is losing 20,000-25,000 soldiers killed per month in Ukraine. Ukraine's March 2026 count of 35,351 Russian KIA/WIA remains the deadliest single month of the war. Russia continues ~1,000 KIA/WIA per day through the week of April 17-21. Finnish President Alexander Stubb cited a 1:5 kill ratio favoring Ukraine, attributing it to Ukraine's drone mass production.
comparables: The total cost is now the most expensive conventional war since WWII — the combined spending has already surpassed the Marshall Plan (in today's dollars, $160B) four times over
At $825M/day, this conflict burns through an Apollo Moon Program's worth of spending (in today's dollars, $280B) every 10 months
Russia's military spending alone ($165B/yr) now exceeds its entire federal healthcare budget for 145 million citizens — by more than 2×

casualties.note: Russia does not publish military casualties. BBC/Mediazona open-source investigation identified ~209,000 Russian KIA by name as of April 10, 2026 (up from 207,552+ on April 3) — a confirmed floor. BBC modelled total Russian KIA at 319,300–461,200 (April 3, 2026). CSIS estimated 1.2M total Russian casualties (killed and wounded) through December 2025; Ukraine's General Staff tallied 1,298,730 by April 1, 2026. UK MoD (June 2025) estimated 250,000 killed. A late February 2026 Western intelligence estimate put Russian total casualties (killed + wounded) at ~1,000,000; Ukrainian figures (250,000–300,000 killed and wounded) from the same Western source. These are lower than CSIS's figures — divergence reflects methodological differences. Ukrainian figures are state-suppressed; Zelenskyy cited 55,000 KIA in Feb 2026, widely considered an undercount. OHCHR civilian deaths: 15,172 confirmed killed, 41,378 injured by January 31, 2026. Russia is cutting planned defense spending by ~7% in 2026 (14.9T vs 16T rubles in 2025), but mid-year budget amendments are likely given higher oil prices from the Iran conflict. March 2026 was Russia's deadliest single month of the war: 35,351 casualties in one month per Ukrainian General Staff (United24 Media, Apr 2026). APRIL 2026 UPDATE: Ukraine's General Staff reports cumulative Russian losses of 1,311,180 troops (Apr 12) and approximately 1,318,000+ by Apr 20. Russia's March 2026 casualties (35,351) marked the deadliest month of the war for Russian forces — a 29% increase on February, with drones accounting for 96% of casualties. Russia lost ~1,000 soldiers per day through mid-April. Russia's replacement rate has fallen behind losses since December 2025 per Ukraine's defense ministry. APRIL 20-21, 2026: Ukraine General Staff cumulative Russian losses ~1,319,270 on April 20 (+1,050 in 24h). NATO Sec Gen Rutte: Russia losing 20,000-25,000 KIA per month in Ukraine. Finnish President Stubb cited a 1:5 kill ratio (Ukraine to Russia) driven by drone operations. Russia's equipment losses on Apr 20: 11,884 tanks, 24,422 AFVs, 40,396 artillery systems cumulatively (Ukrainian General Staff).

name: Gaza / Middle East Wars
parties: Israel + U.S. vs. Hamas / Hezbollah / Houthis
sources: Bank of Israel (direct defense costs ~$77B through 2025); AA.com — war expenses $76.3B in 2 years (Oct 2025); Brown University Costs of War 2025 — U.S. military aid $21.7B; Breaking Defense Mar 2026 — Israel $45B defense budget; Haaretz Jan 2026; WAFA (Apr 13, 2026 — Gaza death toll surpasses 72,333; 172,202 injured); WAFA (Apr 15, 2026 — 72,344 killed / 172,242 injured per Gaza MoH); onaquietday.org (Apr 15, 2026 — 8 more killed by Israeli fire, death toll nears 72,350); UN Women (Apr 17, 2026 — more than 38,000 women and girls killed in Gaza between October 2023 and December 2025, including 22,000+ women and 16,000+ girls); UN.org (Apr 17, 2026 — press release, UN Women on Gaza casualties); WAFA (Apr 18, 2026 — Gaza death toll 72,549); Business Upturn (Apr 18, 2026 — 72,549 dead; 773 killed since ceasefire began); OHCHR (Apr 10, 2026 — Türk: Palestinians across Gaza unsafe six months on from ceasefire); NPR (Apr 16, 2026 — six months after ceasefire with Israel, people in Gaza say recovery hasn't even begun); Antiwar.com / News From Antiwar (Apr 2-20, 2026 — ongoing ceasefire violations); Al Jazeera (Apr 17, 2026 — Israeli forces kill Palestinians in Gaza despite ceasefire); Statista (2026 — monthly Gaza fatalities); GlobalSecurity (Apr 19, 2026 — Israeli attacks kill seven as 2,400 ceasefire violations recorded); Business Upturn (Apr 2026 — Gaza casualties climb to 72,549 with 773 killed since ceasefire); MSF (Apr 2026 — 'This is not a ceasefire': life in Gaza continues to be suffocated six months on)
summary: Gaza ceasefire reached mid-October 2025 after 2 years of conflict. Israel direct defense costs: ~$77B through end-2025 (Bank of Israel); U.S. military aid: $21.7B (Brown Univ). Total theater cost ~$106B to date. Post-ceasefire rate of $100M/day reflects ongoing Houthi exchanges, residual IDF brigades in Gaza, and Lebanon border operations. Iran escalation costs (Op. Epic Fury, Feb 2026+) are tracked separately. Israel approved $45B 2026 defense budget (Breaking Defense, Mar 2026). APRIL 15, 2026: Gaza MoH via WAFA reported death toll of 72,344 killed and 172,242 injured. Eight more Palestinians killed on April 15 alone in continued Israeli attacks. Since ceasefire began (October 10, 2025): 765 Palestinians killed and 2,140 injured. 760 bodies recovered from rubble since ceasefire. Israel has bombed Gaza on the vast majority of days since the ceasefire began. Thousands remain under rubble. The ongoing pace of killing through the "ceasefire" period continues to draw international condemnation. APRIL 18-21, 2026 UPDATE: Gaza Ministry of Health cumulative toll: 72,549 killed, 172,274 wounded as of April 18, 2026. At least 773 Palestinians killed and 2,171 wounded since the Oct 10-11, 2025 Trump-brokered ceasefire took effect — rescue teams have recovered 761 bodies from beneath destroyed buildings. Six-month ceasefire milestone (Apr 10-11): UN Human Rights Chief Türk said Palestinians across Gaza remain unsafe, with ongoing airstrikes, gunfire, and shelling despite the ceasefire. In the 24 hours preceding April 18, health authorities received eight bodies (seven newly killed, one recovered from rubble) and 24 wounded. Israel-backed militias have begun clashing with Hamas in several areas (Antiwar.com, Apr 20). Around 40% of ceasefire-period casualties are women and children.
comparables: Total military cost ($76B+) exceeds the entire GDP of Jordan — spent fighting over a territory of 2.3 million people
The 2-year war bill could have funded complete Gaza reconstruction (estimated $40–50B by the UN) almost twice over — yet rebuilding has not begun
Israel's peak daily spending during active operations ($350M+/day) exceeded the entire annual UN Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA) budget in under 3 days

casualties.note: Cumulative MoH death toll: 72,344 as of April 15, 2026 (WAFA). Six-month ceasefire milestone reached April 10, 2026: at least 765 Palestinians killed and 2,140+ injured since the ceasefire began (WAFA/MoH, Apr 15, 2026). Israel bombed Gaza on 36 of the 40 days between February 28 and April 8, 2026 (the period of the US-Iran war), killing at least 107 and injuring 342 in that window alone (Al Jazeera, Apr 9, 2026). Since the ceasefire began (October 10, 2025), Israel has violated it at least 2,073 times through airstrikes, artillery, and direct shootings (documented through March 18, 2026). Israel has allowed only 4,999 of 23,400 promised trucks into Gaza — just 21% of the agreed humanitarian deliveries. OHCHR High Commissioner Türk said on April 10 that ‘Palestinians across Gaza [are] unsafe six months on from ceasefire announcement.’ Israel’s implementation of the humanitarian protocol has not exceeded 38% of agreed levels. Independent analyses (Al Jazeera, Feb 2026) citing verified loss data put the total at over 75,000. The Lancet Global Health (2025 field survey) estimated ~75,200 violent deaths through January 5, 2025 alone — suggesting the MoH confirmed total is conservative. UNRWA has recorded 391 staff killed in Gaza since Oct 7, 2023 (to March 31, 2026). The Lancet (Jul 2024) estimated up to 186,000 total deaths including indirect causes. Since the ceasefire (Oct 10, 2025), Israel has violated it thousands of times through near-daily attacks. Gaza MoH figures are considered credible by WHO and UN. APRIL 17, 2026: A UN Women report released April 17 found that more than 38,000 women and girls were killed in Gaza between October 2023 and December 2025, including over 22,000 women and 16,000 girls — providing the most comprehensive gender-disaggregated accounting to date. An IDF official in January 2026 acknowledged the Israeli army accepted that about 70,000 people had been killed in Gaza during the war — indirectly confirming the Gaza MoH/MoH-adjacent counts. APRIL 18, 2026 UPDATE: Cumulative Gaza MoH toll: 72,549 killed, 172,274 wounded. Since ceasefire began Oct 10-11, 2025: 773 killed and 2,171 wounded; 761 bodies recovered from destroyed buildings. Approximately 40% of ceasefire-period casualties are women and children. UN and scholars estimate the actual toll is significantly higher than MoH figures — Lancet's November 2024 survey estimated 64,260 violent deaths to Jan 5 2025, 41% above MoH at that time; true total now could be well above 100,000. Six-month OHCHR milestone (Apr 10): UN Human Rights Chief Türk said Palestinians across Gaza remain 'unsafe' with continuing strikes despite ceasefire. Hamas also now clashing with Israeli-backed militias (Antiwar.com, Apr 20). APRIL 19, 2026: GlobalSecurity/PressTV reported ~2,400 Israeli ceasefire violations from October 10, 2025 through April 14, 2026 — 921 civilian shooting incidents, 97 raids beyond the 'yellow line', 1,109 bombings/shellings, and 273 property demolitions. Al Jazeera reported Israeli attacks killed 'several' Palestinians in two days (Apr 16-17); seven killed in new wave on April 19.

name: Sudan Civil War
parties: Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) vs. Rapid Support Forces (RSF)
sources: Nuba Reports; Taylor & Francis conflict cost study (Nov 2025); SIPRI; ACLED; Doctors Without Borders / MSF (Apr 2026 — three years of war, shattered lifelines); UN OCHA (Apr 2026 — 500+ civilians killed by drone strikes Jan 1–Mar 15 2026); Al Jazeera (Apr 2, 2026 — RSF attack on Al Jabalain Hospital: 10 dead, 7 medical staff among victims); CFR Global Conflict Tracker Sudan (Apr 2026); Daily Sabah (Apr 2026 — fourth year of war, mounting deaths); UN OCHA / UN aid chief Tom Fletcher (Apr 2026 — nearly 700 civilians killed in drone strikes in Q1 2026); ACLED (2024 — conflict deaths exceeded 25,000 for first time); Sudan government / Reuters (Jan 11, 2026 — government returns to Khartoum); Chad government (Feb 23, 2026 — Chad shuts eastern border with Sudan indefinitely); Berlin donors conference (Mar 2026 — faltering peace talks); Al Jazeera (Apr 16, 2026 — After three years of war, Sudan army and RSF locked in military impasse); WBUR Here & Now (Apr 20, 2026 — Sudan's civil war enters 4th year); UN data (Feb 2026 — 11,300 civilians killed in 2025, triple 2024); Wikipedia Sudanese civil war 2023-present (Apr 2026); IRC (Apr 2026 — crisis in Sudan, what is happening); HRW World Report 2026 Sudan
summary: War erupted April 2023 between SAF and RSF. Nuba Reports: $3-8M/day in direct military spending; $8M/day used as upper-bound estimate. SAF recaptured Khartoum March 2025. 12M+ displaced. Pre-war military budget ~$1B/yr; wartime spending roughly tripled. FOURTH YEAR UPDATE (Apr 2026): As the war enters its fourth year (April 2023–April 2026), Sudan remains the world's largest humanitarian crisis. The government returned to Khartoum on January 11, 2026. Chad shut its eastern border with Sudan indefinitely on February 23, 2026 following repeated incursions by Sudanese armed groups. Drone warfare has intensified dramatically in early 2026 — UN aid chief Tom Fletcher reported that nearly 700 civilians were killed in drone strikes in Q1 2026 alone. In 2024, conflict deaths exceeded 25,000 for the first time. The UN has appealed for $2.9B for 2026 response, currently only 16% covered. Berlin donors conference (March 2026) attempted to revive faltering peace talks. 28.9 million people are now acutely food insecure (up from 24.6M in previous estimates). The western Darfur region remains under RSF control; a UN fact-finding mission identified "hallmarks of genocide" earlier in 2026. More people are living in famine conditions in Sudan than the rest of the world combined. FOURTH-YEAR MILESTONE (Apr 15, 2026): Sudan's civil war has now entered its fourth year with SAF and RSF locked in a grinding war of attrition (Al Jazeera, Apr 16). The country is split roughly in two — SAF controls the east, RSF the west. In early 2026, fighting intensified in the Kordofan region with near-daily drone strikes on markets, health facilities, and residential areas. UN data (Feb 2026): 11,300 civilians killed in 2025 — triple the 2024 figure. Estimates now range from 61,000 (conservative) to 400,000+ (former U.S. envoy). 11 million IDPs inside Sudan, plus 4 million refugees in Chad/Egypt/South Sudan. 26 million face acute food insecurity; 33.7 million require humanitarian assistance.
comparables: The daily military cost ($8M) equals the annual income of more than 10,000 Sudanese citizens — a country where GDP per capita is under $800
Sudan's wartime military spending ($3B+/yr) is roughly 15× what the government spent on healthcare before the conflict
The conflict has created the world's largest displacement crisis — at a cost that equates to $250 per displaced person per year in military spending alone

casualties.source: ACLED (documented fatalities to Nov 2024: 28,700+); OHCHR (Jan–Jun 2025: 3,384 civilians); UN OCHA; Sudan Doctors' Network; factually.co verified tally (Nov 2025); WFP (2026 — 24.6M facing acute hunger); Yale researchers warning (2026); IRC (2026 — 150,000+ killed); IRC displacement figure (2026 — 13.6M displaced); PBS NewsHour (Mar 2026 — Khartoum suffering); defconlevel.com Sudan tracker (Apr 2026); Al Jazeera (Jan–Apr 2026 — RSF drone strikes Kartala, Singa); AcademicJobs/Wikipedia (Sudan Civil War Escalation 2026 — SAF strikes Al Zorg, 64 civilians killed); UN OCHA (Apr 2026 — 500+ civilians killed by drone strikes Jan 1–Mar 15 2026); Al Jazeera (Apr 2, 2026 — RSF attack on Al Jabalain Hospital); Doctors Without Borders (Apr 2026 — three years of war); Daily Sabah (Apr 2026 — fourth year of war); UN OCHA / Tom Fletcher (Apr 2026 — 700 civilians killed in drone strikes Q1 2026); ACLED (2024 — conflict deaths exceeded 25,000 first time); Reuters (Jan 11, 2026 — government returns to Khartoum); AFP/Reuters (Feb 23, 2026 — Chad closes border)
casualties.note: Documented ACLED figures (28,700+) and aggregate estimates diverge sharply — the gap reflects unrecorded violence, famine, and disease deaths. Former U.S. envoy for Sudan stated in 2026 that as many as 400,000 may have been killed; the IRC puts the toll at 150,000+; the UN now estimates around 40,000 killed. A London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine report (Nov 2024) estimated 61,000+ deaths in Khartoum State alone for just April 2023–June 2024. Researchers note they 'can't responsibly give a number' because 'many who would do the counting are dead or displaced.' Yale researchers warned 2026 could be the conflict's bloodiest year yet. Drone strikes on hospitals, markets, and residential areas in Kordofan continue into April 2026 — April incidents include RSF drone strikes on Kartala (5 killed) and Singa (27 killed, 73 wounded — targeting a SAF base during a government meeting). SAF airstrikes on Al Zorg (Hemedti's hometown) in early April 2026 killed up to 64 civilians and destroyed the town's hospital, also killing two of Hemedti's relatives and a local tribal leader. The Sudan-origin drone that struck Chad in March 2026 (killing 17) underscores cross-border spillover. UN now reports 14 million displaced — world's largest displacement crisis. The WFP reports 24.6 million in acute hunger. UN OCHA reported (April 2026) that drone attacks alone killed over 500 civilians across Sudan from January 1 to March 15, 2026 — an alarming rate averaging ~9 civilians per day from aerial strikes. On April 2, 2026 an RSF attack on Al Jabalain Hospital killed 10 people, including 7 medical staff — one of multiple documented attacks on health facilities in the first weeks of April. Total displacement as of early 2026: 8.6 million internally displaced and 3 million refugees (early 2026 UN estimates), making this the world's largest displacement crisis — the IRC puts the total at over 11.6 million combined. FOURTH YEAR (Apr 2026): UN aid chief Tom Fletcher stated nearly 700 civilians were killed in drone strikes in Q1 2026 alone. 2024 was the deadliest year since the war began — conflict deaths exceeded 25,000 for the first time. A UN fact-finding mission identified "hallmarks of genocide" in Darfur. Food insecurity has worsened to 28.9 million acutely food insecure (up from 24.6M). Sudan accounts for 10% of all global humanitarian needs. The Sudan government returned to Khartoum on January 11, 2026. Chad closed its eastern border with Sudan indefinitely on February 23, 2026. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: UN data (Feb 2026) documented 11,300 civilians killed in 2025 alone — nearly triple the 2024 figure — with thousands more missing or unidentified. The conflict death toll estimate gap has widened: 61,000 (Nov 2024 LSHTM) to 150,000+ (IRC) to 400,000+ (former U.S. envoy). 11M IDPs inside Sudan; 4M refugees in Chad/Egypt/South Sudan; 26M acute food insecurity; 33.7M require humanitarian assistance.
