name: Myanmar Civil War
parties: Military Junta (SAC) vs. People's Defence Force + ethnic armed groups
sources: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database 2024 (April 2025 release); IISS Military Balance 2026; SIPRI press release Apr 2025; Al Jazeera (Mar 27, 2026 — who is fighting in Myanmar's multi-front civil war); IISS Myanmar Conflict Map (Apr 2026 — junta controls <40% of townships); Conflict.sbs Myanmar Civil War 2026 report; FDD (Apr 15, 2026 — new resistance alliance built to win Myanmar's civil war); The Diplomat (Apr 2026 — Three Indian nationals killed in Myanmar civil war); HRW World Report 2026 (Myanmar chapter); The Diplomat (Apr 2026 — three Indian nationals killed in Myanmar civil war); Al Jazeera (Mar 27, 2026 — Who is fighting in Myanmar's multi-front civil war); UN OCHA (Apr 2026 — 75,000+ conflict-related deaths)
summary: Junta seized power Feb 2021. SIPRI 2024 (released Apr 2025): Myanmar military spending surged 66% to $5.0B/yr — highest rate of increase in Asia-Pacific and 6.8% of GDP — equating to ~$13.7M/day. Previous $2.5B/yr figure predated the 66% surge. Military now consumes 29% of government expenditure. Resistance controls significant territory. Figures reflect junta spending only. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: A new resistance alliance has been formed — announced April 15, 2026 (FDD) — representing the most politically and militarily aligned resistance coalition since the 2021 coup. The Arakan Army's inclusion is significant; it controls most of Rakhine State and has established proto-state structures along Myanmar's western coast. SHAM ELECTIONS: The junta held sham elections in three phases (December 28, 2025 – January 25, 2026), conducted in only 263 of 330 townships. The UN High Commissioner for Human Rights stated the process "failed to respect the fundamental human rights" of Myanmar's citizens. Three Indian nationals were killed in Myanmar in late March 2026 in incidents involving a rebel group aligned with the military. After the 2025 earthquake, the SAC announced a ceasefire but then conducted more than 550 attacks in the following two months. Myanmar's economy has lost nearly $100 billion since the coup.
comparables: The junta now spends 29% of all government revenue on this war — more than education, health, and infrastructure combined
Annual military spending ($5B) is more than 10× Myanmar's entire public healthcare budget in one of Asia's poorest countries
The $13.7M/day rate means Myanmar's military spends more in a week than the entire annual UNICEF budget for the country

casualties.source: ACLED (96,000+ total killed, 2026); AAPP (Assistance Association for Political Prisoners, Apr 2025 — 6,486 confirmed civilian deaths); UN News (Jan 2026 — 5.2M displaced); OHCHR (Jan 2025 four-year report); HRW World Report 2026; Al Jazeera (Mar 27, 2026); Myanmar civil war Wikipedia (territory control estimates); FDD (Apr 15, 2026 — new resistance alliance); The Diplomat (Apr 2026 — Indian nationals killed); UN High Commissioner for Human Rights (Jan 2026 — sham elections assessment)
casualties.note: Myanmar's military blocks independent access. AAPP tracks confirmed civilian deaths via open-source documentation — widely considered a floor. ACLED's broader estimate of 96,000+ total killed (all parties, all causes) reflects the full scale of the conflict since the Feb 2021 coup. The junta has escalated airstrikes on civilian targets through 2025–26, including strikes on schools, hospitals, and IDP camps. A January 2026 airstrike in Bhamo Township, Kachin State killed up to 50 civilians. As of early 2026, the junta controls only 21% of Myanmar's territory, while resistance forces hold 42% (2024 investigation). The UN estimates 5.2 million people are now internally displaced (UN News, Jan 2026 — up from 3.6M in prior estimates). The March 2025 earthquake compounded the crisis: the military continued airstrikes in the immediate aftermath. Despite territorial losses, some analysts suggest the balance is beginning to tilt back toward the junta in certain regions. UN FIGURES (2026): The United Nations now reports the conflict has resulted in more than 75,000 total deaths and displaced over 3 million civilians — however, UN access is severely restricted and the ACLED figure of 96,000+ is considered more comprehensive. Territory assessment (early 2026): credible monitoring organizations estimate the junta controls fewer than 40% of Myanmar's townships (IISS Conflict Map), a dramatic collapse from near-total control in 2021. A professor at Loughborough University stated the junta 'may now be in a death spiral' given strategic reversals, territorial losses, and economic decline. The junta retook Kyaukme (Shan State) in October 2025 — a rare success. APRIL 2026: A new resistance alliance has formed, potentially representing a turning point in the war. The junta's sham elections (Dec 28, 2025 – Jan 25, 2026) were condemned by the UN Human Rights High Commissioner as failing "to respect the fundamental human rights" of Myanmar citizens — held in only 263/330 townships. Three Indian nationals killed in Myanmar in late March 2026. Despite the March 2025 earthquake ceasefire declaration, the junta conducted 550+ attacks in the subsequent two months. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: UN reports over 75,000 total deaths and over 3 million displaced. AAPP figures remain a conservative floor last widely published through April 2025; ACLED's 96,000+ figure remains the broader estimate. The junta's control has eroded to below 40% of townships (IISS, early 2026). Desertion rates remain high and the Tatmadaw's effective combat strength is significantly below its nominal force.

spendChart.note: Junta spending only; PDF/resistance funding undisclosed

parties.name: Military Junta (SAC)
parties.explainer: SIPRI 2024 (released April 2025): Myanmar military spending surged 66% to $5.0B/yr — highest rate of increase in Asia-Pacific, consuming 29% of government expenditure and 6.8% of GDP. Previous estimate of $2.5B/yr predated this surge.

parties.name: People's Defence Force + EAOs
parties.explainer: The PDF and allied Ethnic Armed Organizations (EAOs) are funded through diaspora donations, local taxation in controlled territories, and in some cases cross-border trade. No centralized budget exists — aggregate spending is not independently verifiable.

name: Sahel / West Africa
parties: Multiple governments vs. JNIM / ISGS (Islamic State Greater Sahara)
sources: SIPRI 2024; ACLED conflict data; IISS Africa report 2025; ACLED (2024 Annual conflict report — conflict deaths exceeded 25,000 first time); Africa Center (Apr 2026 — widening scope of militant Islamist threat); ADF Magazine (Apr 2026 — violence against civilians surges in Niger's Tillabéri region); JNIM/ISSP attack on Niamey international airport (Feb 2026); FDD (Apr 15, 2026 — new resistance alliance, JNIM spillover to Nigeria noted); ACLED (Apr 2026 — conflict intensifies and instability spreads beyond Burkina Faso, Mali, Niger); US News / Reuters (Apr 2, 2026 — Burkina, Mali troops kill more civilians than jihadists); Africa Center (Apr 2026 — widening scope of militant Islamist threat); Africanews (Apr 20, 2026 — Africa's shifting security map, ten years on); Pravda Mali (Apr 18, 2026 — summary of fighting in Sahel Apr 4–17)
summary: Jihadist insurgency across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad. French withdrew 2023; Russian Africa Corps present. Combined government military budgets ~$1.5B/yr. ~$4M/day blended estimate. 10,000+ civilian deaths annually (ACLED). 2026 UPDATE: In 2024, conflict deaths in the region exceeded 25,000 for the first time, with JNIM responsible for ~50% of Burkina Faso fatalities. The Sahel accounts for more than half of all global terrorism-related deaths (GTI 2026). BURKINA FASO (March 2026): JNIM attacked special police in Yamba (March 6), killing 30 police; further JNIM attack in Dourtenga (March 15) killed 9 VDPs. NIGER (Feb 2026): ISSP launched an unprecedented attack on the international airport in Niamey — a dramatic escalation. Niger's Tillabéri region saw surging civilian violence through April 2026 (ADF Magazine). CROSS-BORDER SPILLOVER: JNIM has now claimed attacks in Nigeria (Kwara/Niger border with Benin), marking a new geographic expansion. MALI (Feb 2026): Former Prime Minister Moussa Mara jailed for "discrediting the state." Niger now accounts for 17% of Sahel fatalities (up from 7% in 2023). APRIL 2026 UPDATE: ACLED confirms in 2025, the Burkinabe military and pro-government Homeland Defence Volunteers militia killed 523 civilians — more than JNIM (339) and ISSP combined. In Mali, government forces + partners killed 3–4× as many civilians as jihadists over the past two years. The violence is largely driven by JNIM (78% of regional fatalities). Burkina Faso accounts for ~50% of all militant Islamist-linked fatalities in the region, Mali ~29%, Niger 17%. In April 2026 alone, major clashes in Niger between Islamic State Sahel Province (IG-C) and JNIM killed at least 50 Islamists from both sides.
comparables: Combined Sahel military spending ($1.5B/yr) exceeds the entire World Food Programme emergency food budget for West Africa — despite the region facing acute food insecurity
France spent more deploying its Barkhane force in its final year than all five Sahel countries combined spent on primary education
The five affected countries have per-capita GDPs of under $1,000 — yet are sustaining military expenditure that consumes up to 20% of government revenue

casualties.source: ACLED 2024 Annual Report; ACLED Africa Overview March 2026; IISS Armed Conflict Survey 2024; HRW / US News (Apr 2, 2026 — government forces killing more civilians than militants); UN OCHA (2025 — 3,737 security incidents, 9,362 deaths); Pravda Burkina Faso (Apr 4, 2026 — Sahel military summary); ACLED Conflict Watchlist 2026; ACLED 2024 Annual Report — record 25,000+ conflict deaths; ADF Magazine (Apr 2026 — Niger Tillabéri civilian violence); Africa Center (Apr 2026 — widening Islamist threat, JNIM spillover to Nigeria); ACLED (Feb 2026 — ISSP Niamey airport attack)
casualties.note: Figures cover Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Chad. UN OCHA recorded 3,737 security incidents resulting in 9,362 deaths in the region in 2025 alone (ACLED/OCHA). The Sahel accounted for more than half of all global terrorism-related deaths in 2025 (GTI 2026). ACLED Conflict Watchlist 2026 notes the region remains the global epicenter of terrorism. A confirmed and alarming finding (HRW / ACLED / US News, April 2, 2026): government and allied forces in Burkina Faso and Mali have killed more than twice as many civilians as Islamist militants since 2023 — in Burkina Faso, government/VDP forces killed 523 civilians in 2025 vs. JNIM/ISSP killing 339; in Mali, the military and Russian Africa Corps killed 918 civilians in 2025 vs. JNIM/ISSP killing 232. JNIM launched a week-long coordinated offensive in February 2026 across eastern and northern Burkina Faso, demonstrating growing operational reach. Through March 21–April 3 (Pravda Mali summary Apr 6, 2026), fighting continued across the region. In early 2026 suspected militants killed 50+ civilians across two attacks in Niger's Tillabéri region. An oil pipeline was blown up in Niger in late March 2026. JNIM remains the primary threat; Islamic State Sahel Province (ISGS) is also active. 2026 UPDATE: Conflict deaths in 2024 exceeded 25,000 for the first time. ISSP launched an unprecedented attack on Niamey international airport in February 2026. JNIM has claimed attacks inside Nigeria for the first time (Kwara/Niger border), marking a new geographic expansion. Niger's share of Sahel fatalities has risen from 7% (2023) to 17%. Violence against civilians is surging in Niger's Tillabéri region (April 2026). APRIL 2026 UPDATE: ACLED confirms in 2025 Burkinabe military + pro-government militia killed 523 civilians vs JNIM 339 — state forces now kill more civilians than jihadists in Burkina Faso and Mali. JNIM is responsible for 78% of regional fatalities. 50+ Islamists killed in clashes between IG-C and JNIM in Niger in April 2026.

spendChart.note: Blended multi-country estimate; France + Russian Africa Corps included

parties.name: Sahel Governments + Partners
parties.explainer: Combined defense budgets of Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Chad total ~$1.5B/yr. France's Barkhane force (~3,000 troops, $600M/yr at peak) withdrew in 2023; Russian Africa Corps (formerly Wagner) moved in at lower cost. The G5 Sahel joint force collapsed in 2023 following the coups.

parties.name: JNIM / ISGS
parties.explainer: JNIM and ISGS are self-financing through taxation of local populations, kidnap-for-ransom, and cross-border smuggling. No reliable expenditure figure exists. JNIM controls territory spanning parts of Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger, generating significant local revenue.

name: DR Congo / M23
parties: DRC Armed Forces (FARDC) + SADC vs. M23 / Rwanda
sources: SIPRI 2023 (DRC +105%); ACLED; UN Group of Experts reports; UN News (Mar 26, 2026 — violence intensifies as UN mission pledges continued support); Global Security / UNSC (Mar 26, 2026 — despite ceasefire, situation continues to deteriorate); ACLED (Apr 2026 — 173 cases conflict-related sexual violence confirmed since Dec 2025); ICG (Apr 2026 — Elusive Peace in the Great Lakes, updated); Washington Accords (Mar 17-18, 2026 — DRC and Rwanda renew commitment to peace implementation); SOS Médias Burundi (Apr 18, 2026 — Burundian troops progress in South Kivu amid M23 clashes); Africa Center (Apr 2026 — risk of regional conflict following fall of Goma and M23 offensive); UN Security Council briefing (Mar 26, 2026); U.S. Department of State (Apr 18, 2026 — Joint Statement on Progress Between DRC and AFC/M23, Montreux); AllAfrica (Apr 19, 2026 — Joint Statement on Ceasefire Meetings in Switzerland); Meyka (Apr 18, 2026 — DRC-M23 Ceasefire: Prisoner Release Deal Signed); Taarifa Rwanda (Apr 19, 2026 — Kinshasa and AFC/M23 Formalise Ceasefire Joint Verification Mechanism); Al Jazeera (Apr 19, 2026 — DRC government, M23 rebels commit to protecting civilians, aid deliveries); Anews (Apr 20, 2026 — DR Congo army accuses M23 of deadly attacks amid peace talks); Xinhua (Apr 18, 2026 — DR Congo gov't-rebels peace talks see progress)
summary: M23 resurgence from late 2022, backed by Rwanda (UN Group of Experts confirmed). DRC military spending surged 105% in 2023 — largest increase of any country globally (SIPRI). DRC defense budget ~$1.5-2B/yr by 2023. ~$5.5M/day blended estimate including SADC forces. MARCH-APRIL 2026 UPDATE: The DRC and Rwanda held a meeting in Washington on March 17-18, 2026, renewing commitments to implement the February 18 ceasefire agreement. The UN welcomed the meeting and concrete steps agreed. However, the UN Security Council was briefed in late March that the situation "continues to deteriorate," with clashes between AFC/M23 and FARDC intensifying, including growing use of offensive drones and GPS jamming/spoofing. ACLED documented 173 confirmed cases of conflict-related sexual violence since December 2025. Violence in eastern DRC in 2025 exceeded 2,500 fatalities in Q1 2025 alone (ACLED). Pro-Congolese Wazalendo forces recaptured some villages in Kalehe district. The Doha Framework peace process relocated to Switzerland for further negotiations in April 2026. The Islamic State has been capitalizing on M23's territorial gains, expanding its footprint in eastern DRC. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: M23 continues to hold Goma, Bukavu, and the Rubaya coltan-mining site. Burundian troops reported moving toward Minembwe via Bibokoboko (Apr 18) amid continued clashes with M23 and Twirwaneho forces. The February 2026 Angola-brokered ceasefire remains fragile — UN briefed that situation 'continues to deteriorate.' Over half a million people displaced from South Kivu alone since Dec 2025. UN estimates at least 2,900 killed in M23's January 2025 Goma offensive (DRC government puts that figure at 2,000). APRIL 17-18, 2026 MONTREUX BREAKTHROUGH: Following five days of negotiations in Montreux, Switzerland (Apr 13-17) co-convened by the U.S., Qatar, Togo (AU mediator), the African Union Commission and Switzerland, DRC and AFC/M23 signed a memorandum of understanding operationalizing the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus (EJVM+) to monitor the permanent ceasefire, and committed to release more than 300 AFC/M23 detainees and 166 government-held prisoners by April 26. The parties made 'substantial progress' toward a protocol on humanitarian access and judicial protection. However, DR Congo's army accused M23 of deadly drone and artillery attacks in the two days after the announcement (Apr 19-20) — the first major test of the new mechanism. No change to ratePerDay yet; counter continues pending verifiable durable de-escalation.
comparables: DRC military spending doubled in a single year (SIPRI 2023) — while per-capita healthcare spending remains around $20/year for 100 million citizens
The conflict zone contains an estimated $24 trillion in untapped mineral wealth — the entire war's cost to date is less than 0.01% of what's at stake underground
At $5.5M/day, the conflict costs more than the DRC's entire annual education budget — every 4 months

casualties.source: ACLED; UN Security Council (Aug 2025, Mar 2026); Human Rights Watch (Jun, Aug 2025); Al Jazeera DRC mapping project (Mar 2025); OHCHR (Aug 2025); African Union (Feb 12, 2026 — ceasefire TOR signing); UN MONUSCO (Mar 2026); Security Council Report April 2026 forecast; Critical Threats Congo War Security Review (Apr 3, 2026); UN News (Mar 11, 2026 — Goma drone attack kills UNICEF staffer); ICG (Apr 2026 — Elusive Peace in the Great Lakes); UN News (Mar 26, 2026 — violence intensifies); Global Security / UNSC (Mar 26, 2026 — situation deteriorates despite ceasefire); Washington Accords briefing (Mar 17-18, 2026); ACLED (Apr 2026 — 173 sexual violence cases confirmed)
casualties.note: On February 11–12, 2026, Angola brokered a ceasefire with Terms of Reference signed. The ceasefire took effect February 18, 2026. However, the UN Security Council was briefed in March 2026 that the situation 'continues to deteriorate.' A March 11, 2026 drone attack in Goma killed one UNICEF staff member and two other civilians — illustrating continued instability despite the ceasefire framework. The Doha Framework for Peace (November 2025) relocated to Switzerland in April 2026 for further negotiations. Despite diplomatic activity, ACLED warns the Islamic State is capitalizing on M23's territorial gains, expanding its footprint in eastern DRC as M23 controls more territory. As of April 1–3, 2026, M23 made limited tactical withdrawals from some positions while still expanding in Lubero, Masisi, and Walikale territories (MONUSCO, April 2026). Pro-Congolese Wazalendo fighters recaptured several villages in Kalehe district (border of North/South Kivu). MONUSCO data (2026): more than 1,000 civilians killed in Ituri and North Kivu since June 2025 alone. Over 500,000 displaced in South Kivu since December 2025. Documented mass atrocities: 773 killed in Goma (Feb 2025); 319+ civilians in Rutshuru (Jul 2025); 400+ in South Kivu (Dec 2025). 7M+ internally displaced (updated from 6.4M); food insecurity projected to affect 26.6M in 2026. The Doha Framework for Peace relocated to Switzerland (April 2026) for further negotiations; the February ceasefire agreement remains unimplemented. MARCH-APRIL 2026 UPDATE: DRC-Rwanda Washington meeting (Mar 17-18) renewed ceasefire commitments, welcomed by the UN. However, the UN Security Council (Mar 26) reported the situation "continues to deteriorate" despite the ceasefire framework. ACLED confirmed 173 cases of conflict-related sexual violence since December 2025. The Doha Framework relocated to Switzerland (April 2026) for further negotiations. Islamic State has been capitalizing on M23 territorial gains in eastern DRC. APRIL 18, 2026: Burundian troops reported progressing towards Minembwe via Bibokoboko in South Kivu (SOS Médias Burundi) amid continued clashes with M23 and Twirwaneho. The Feb 18 ceasefire remains fragile despite the March 17-18 Washington meeting commitments. Over 500,000 displaced from South Kivu since Dec 2025. APRIL 17-18, 2026: DRC and AFC/M23 signed a memorandum of understanding in Montreux, Switzerland, operationalizing the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus (EJVM+), committing to release 300+ AFC/M23 detainees and 166 government-held prisoners by April 26, and advancing a humanitarian access/judicial protection protocol. The ICGLR co-signed. Drone and artillery attacks were reported in eastern DRC within 48 hours of the agreement (Apr 19-20), with the Congolese army accusing M23 of violations — durability of the Montreux framework is an open question.

spendChart.note: DRC + SADC coalition; Rwanda officially denies involvement

parties.name: DRC + SADC Forces (FARDC)
parties.explainer: DRC military spending surged 105% in 2023 — the largest single-year increase of any country globally (SIPRI), driven by the M23 resurgence. DRC defense budget reached ~$1.5–2B/yr by 2023. SADC's SAMIM regional force deployed June 2021 and withdrew July 2024, adding additional coalition costs.

parties.name: M23 / Rwanda (RDF)
parties.explainer: Rwanda officially denies direct military involvement, but UN Group of Experts has confirmed Rwanda Defence Force (RDF) troops embedded with M23. Rwandan military spending ($547M/yr, SIPRI) does not disclose DRC-specific costs. M23 receives weapons, logistics, and personnel from Rwanda — the financial quantum is not independently auditable.

name: The Twelve-Day War (Israel–Iran)
parties: Israel + U.S. vs. Iran
sources: New Arab — Israel faces $12B losses after 12-day war; TRT World — total U.S. + Israel + Iran costs; Al Jazeera Centre for Studies — Twelve Days of Inferno analysis; Britannica — 12-Day War entry; House of Commons Library CBP-10292 (Impacts of June 2025 strikes)
summary: On June 13, 2025, Israel launched a massive air campaign against Iranian nuclear facilities, military bases, and IRGC infrastructure — the IDF's largest single operation, deploying 200+ fighter jets across 5 strike waves targeting 100+ sites. Iran retaliated with 550 ballistic missiles and 1,000 drones. Trump announced a ceasefire on June 23, 2025, naming it the 'Twelve-Day War.' Israel's direct losses estimated at $12B; total combined costs (Israel + U.S. support + Iranian response) estimated at $18B over 12 days (~$1.5B/day). This operation served as the tactical blueprint for Op. Epic Fury (Feb 2026).
comparables: At $1.5B/day, this was the costliest 12-day conflict in recorded history — more expensive per day than the peak of the first Gulf War
The total cost ($18B) exceeds the Manhattan Project in today's dollars ($28B) — spent in under two weeks
Israel launched 200+ jets across 5 strike waves; at average sortie costs, the air campaign alone consumed more than the IDF's entire annual equipment procurement budget

casualties.source: House of Commons Library CBP-10292; Al Jazeera Centre for Studies; Middle East Monitor; Iranian state media
casualties.note: Iran did not publish official military casualty figures. Estimates are derived from strike damage assessments and open-source intelligence.

spendChart.note: Israel direct losses ~$12B; U.S. support ~$6B over 12 days

parties.name: Israel
parties.explainer: The New Arab estimated Israel's direct military losses at $12B over 12 days — comprising munitions expenditure, air defense interceptions (Iran fired 550 ballistic missiles and 1,000 drones), and operational costs for 200+ jet sorties across 5 strike waves. IDF's largest single operation in its history.

parties.name: United States
parties.explainer: U.S. support included positioning carrier strike groups in the eastern Mediterranean and Red Sea, providing real-time intelligence sharing, and pre-positioning munitions. TRT World and Al Jazeera Centre for Studies estimated total U.S. support costs at ~$6B across the 12-day operation.

parties.name: Iran
parties.explainer: Iran's response costs — 550 ballistic missiles and 1,000 drone launches, nationwide air defense activation, IRGC operations — are not publicly disclosed. Iran's total annual IRGC budget is itself classified. No reliable figure can be modelled from open sources.

name: Yemen War
parties: Saudi-led coalition vs. Houthi forces (Ansar Allah)
sources: Wilson Center Dec 2015 ($200M/day peak); GAO 2022 ($54.6B US support 2015-2021); SIPRI; Washington Times (Apr 9, 2026 — Houthis hold Bab al-Mandab Strait, next chokepoint on oil commerce); Security Council Report (Apr 2026 — Yemen briefing and consultations); Wikipedia 2026 Houthi strikes on Israel; ACLED Middle East Monitor (Apr 2026)
summary: Saudi-led coalition intervened March 2015. Wilson Center estimated $200M/day at peak (Dec 2015). GAO confirmed $54.6B in U.S. military support to Saudi Arabia and UAE, 2015-2021. ~$67M/day is a blended 8-year average accounting for high early intensity and reduced tempo post-2022 UN truce. UN-brokered truce April 2023. APRIL 2026 NOTE: Formally in UN-brokered truce since April 2023, but Houthis have resumed active operations: at least 8 barrages launched against Israel since March 28, 2026 (all intercepted, none causing casualties). The April 8 U.S.-Iran ceasefire brokered by Pakistan did not name Houthi obligations or set Yemen conditions. Houthi leader Abdulmalik al-Houthi (Apr 9) called the ceasefire a 'great victory' for Iran but said de-escalation won't succeed unless Israel ceases Lebanon attacks. Houthis have begun screening ships in Red Sea by political identity — applying Iran's Hormuz formula to Bab al-Mandab. Yemeni yellow truce technically holds; counter remains frozen at April 2023.
comparables: The U.S. supplied $54.6B in confirmed military support (GAO) — enough to have funded Yemen's entire economy for a decade at pre-war GDP levels
Saudi Arabia spent more on this conflict annually than on education for its 35 million citizens — for 8 consecutive years
Total cost ($195B+) approaches the entire GDP of Qatar — the country that ultimately helped broker the ceasefire

casualties.source: ACLED; UN OCHA (377,000 est. by end-2021); Yemen Data Project; UNHCR
casualties.note: The UN estimated 377,000 total deaths by end-2021 — the majority from indirect causes: famine, disease, and collapse of healthcare infrastructure. Direct combat deaths are a fraction of the total human cost. APRIL 2026 NOTE: Houthi strikes on Israel resumed post-Iran war (≥8 barrages since Mar 28, all intercepted, no casualties). Casualty count remains frozen at 2021 UN estimate; no major official update.

entries.label: Total deaths — direct + indirect (UN est. to 2021)

spendChart.note: Saudi-led coalition + U.S. support; Houthi spending undisclosed

parties.name: Saudi-Led Coalition
parties.explainer: Saudi Arabia and UAE led the coalition with an estimated $5–7B/yr in direct military costs at peak. Saudi defense spending grew from $81B (2015) to $86B (2016) — the Yemen campaign consumed an estimated 10–15% of that budget. UAE withdrew most forces by 2019; Saudi costs declined thereafter.

parties.name: U.S. Military Support
parties.explainer: GAO confirmed $54.6B in U.S. military support to Saudi Arabia and UAE for Yemen operations, 2015–2021 — comprising arms sales, mid-air refueling (until 2018), and intelligence sharing. Biden paused offensive arms sales in 2021; support effectively ended by 2022.

parties.name: Houthis (Ansar Allah)
parties.explainer: Houthi funding comes primarily from Iran (estimated $100M+/yr in weapons and support per U.S. Treasury designations) and captured Yemeni state assets. The group has no publicly disclosed budget. Missile and drone production costs benefit from Iranian technology transfer.

name: Ethiopia / Tigray War
parties: Ethiopian federal forces + Eritrea vs. TPLF / Tigray Defence Forces
sources: SIPRI (Ethiopia budget $1.03B in 2022, +111%); East African / SIPRI Feb 2022; Oxford Initiative on Peace and Recovery in Ethiopia
summary: War began Nov 2020. Ethiopia's military budget doubled to $1.03B/yr at peak (SIPRI 2022). Infrastructure damage assessed at $22.7B (Ethiopian government). ~$2.8M/day reflects wartime expenditure increase above pre-war baseline, averaged over 2-year conflict. Pretoria ceasefire Nov 2, 2022. Up to 600,000 civilians killed (researchers).
comparables: Ethiopia doubled its military budget to fight this war while simultaneously experiencing the world's largest humanitarian crisis of 2021
Infrastructure damage alone ($22.7B, Ethiopian government estimate) exceeded direct military spending by more than 8× — and reconstruction has barely begun
Up to 600,000 civilians were killed — making this one of the deadliest conflicts since WWII, at a military cost of under $3M/day

casualties.source: Ghent University (2023 peer-reviewed estimate); OHCHR; Amnesty International; Ethiopian Disaster Risk Management Commission
casualties.note: One of the most under-reported conflicts of the modern era. Ghent University's peer-reviewed estimate of 300,000–500,000 deaths (including famine and disease) is the most cited academic figure. Some researchers place the total above 600,000.

spendChart.note: Ethiopian federal + Eritrean spending; Eritrea undisclosed

parties.name: Ethiopia + Eritrea
parties.explainer: Ethiopia's military budget doubled to $1.03B/yr at peak (SIPRI 2022), driven by the Tigray campaign. Eritrea deployed forces but does not publish defense expenditure. Combined wartime spending is estimated at the equivalent of $2.8M/day — roughly the increase above Ethiopia's pre-war military baseline.

parties.name: TPLF / Tigray Defence Forces
parties.explainer: TPLF/TDF funding came from local taxation, captured federal equipment, and diaspora support — none of which is audited. The TDF fielded an estimated 250,000 fighters at peak, suggesting substantial expenditure, but no independent cost figure exists.

name: Libya Civil War
parties: GNA / GNU vs. LNA (Haftar) + foreign backers (Turkey, UAE, Russia, Egypt)
sources: SIPRI; IISS; Stockholm Forum on Peace and Development
summary: Second Libyan Civil War began 2014. Multiple foreign powers: Turkey backed GNA (~$2B arms/forces), UAE/Russia/Egypt backed LNA (Haftar). ~$8M/day blended estimate covering all parties including foreign military presence. UN-brokered ceasefire Oct 2020; situation remains frozen with divided government.
comparables: At least 6 foreign nations funded proxy forces in Libya — with Turkey, UAE, and Russia each spending more annually than Libya's own oil revenue in low-output years
The total cost ($17.5B over 6 years) is less than what Saudi Arabia spent on its military in a single month during the same period
Libya has some of Africa's largest oil reserves — yet the war cost more than the country earned from oil exports in its lowest-production years

casualties.source: ACLED; UNSMIL (UN Support Mission in Libya); Libya Body Count
casualties.note: The chaotic multi-faction nature of the conflict and lack of central record-keeping makes precise figures impossible. Most casualties were combatants from multiple factions.

spendChart.note: Multi-proxy conflict; Turkey, UAE, Russia, Egypt costs estimated

parties.name: GNA / GNU + Turkey
parties.explainer: Turkey committed an estimated $2B+ in arms, drones, and military personnel to back the GNA (later GNU). Turkish involvement escalated significantly in 2019–2020 with the deployment of Syrian mercenaries and Bayraktar TB2 drones — the first large-scale combat use of armed drones in the region.

parties.name: LNA (Haftar) + UAE/Russia/Egypt
parties.explainer: UAE provided Haftar's LNA with advanced weaponry including Chinese-made drones, in violation of the UN arms embargo. Russia deployed Wagner Group mercenaries (estimated 1,000–2,000 at peak). Egypt provided air support and training. Combined proxy spending estimated at $5–10B over the conflict — none publicly audited.

name: Syrian Civil War
parties: Assad government + Russia + Iran vs. opposition + ISIS + U.S. coalition
sources: SIPRI; Brown University Costs of War; Syrian Observatory for Human Rights; Watson Institute
summary: 13-year civil war. At peak (2013-2016): Assad forces + Russia (~$4M/day) + Iran + U.S. coalition (~$14M/day for ISIS campaign) exceeded $50M/day combined. ~$35M/day is a conflict-lifetime blended average accounting for reduced tempo post-2019. Assad regime fell December 8, 2024.
comparables: The 13-year conflict destroyed more than $400B in Syrian economic output — roughly 7× the total direct military cost tracked here
World Bank estimates $400B is needed to rebuild Syria — the international community has pledged less than 10% of that since 2011
At peak spending ($50M+/day in 2015-16), the war burned through a NASA annual budget ($24.8B) every 18 months — while Syrian GDP collapsed by 60%

casualties.source: Syrian Observatory for Human Rights (SOHR); UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs; Brown University Costs of War
casualties.note: SOHR is the most comprehensive single tracker, though its methodology is disputed. The UN suspended official death counts in 2014 citing data quality; independent estimates range from 350,000 to 600,000+.

spendChart.note: Assad regime + Russia vs. US-led coalition; opposition/ISIS undisclosed

parties.name: Assad Regime + Russia + Iran
parties.explainer: Russia's direct military intervention (from Sept 2015) cost an estimated $4–6M/day at peak. Iran spent an estimated $6B+/yr supporting Assad through IRGC advisors, Hezbollah deployment, and weapons transfers (CSIS). Assad's own military budget declined as his army shrank from 300,000 to ~100,000.

parties.name: U.S.-Led Coalition
parties.explainer: Operation Inherent Resolve (Aug 2014–2019 in Syria) cost $24.7B in confirmed U.S. spending (CRS). At peak, the Pentagon reported ~$14M/day in U.S. costs alone. UK, France, Australia, and others added further coalition contributions. Costs sharply declined after ISIS's territorial defeat in March 2019.

parties.name: Opposition / ISIS / Kurds
parties.explainer: The Syrian opposition was funded by Gulf states (Qatar, Saudi Arabia), Turkey, and the CIA's Timber Sycamore program (~$1B total). ISIS self-funded through oil, extortion, and taxes on occupied territories at an estimated $1–2M/day at peak. No aggregate auditable figure exists.

name: Afghanistan War (2011-2021)
parties: U.S. / NATO / Afghan forces vs. Taliban / al-Qaeda
sources: Brown University Costs of War Project; SIGAR; Congressional Research Service; SIPRI
summary: Tracking from 2011 to U.S. withdrawal Aug 2021. Brown University Costs of War: total U.S. cost ~$2.3T over full conflict (2001-2021). ~$116M/day reflects 2011-2021 average of ~$42B/yr in direct DoD war appropriations plus coalition. Peak spending ~$120B/yr (2010-2012). Taliban retook Kabul Aug 15, 2021.
comparables: Brown University estimates the full lifecycle cost at $2.3T (2001–2021, including veteran care) — roughly equivalent to the entire annual GDP of Italy, the world's 8th largest economy
The U.S. spent $116M/day for 10 years in a country whose entire GDP never exceeded $21B/yr — spending more in 6 months than Afghanistan produced in a year
Peak U.S. spending ($120B/yr, 2010-12) exceeded the entire NASA budget nearly 5× — every single year — yet produced no lasting strategic outcome

casualties.source: U.S. Department of Defense (confirmed U.S./coalition deaths); SIGAR; Brown University Costs of War; UNAMA (civilian deaths)
casualties.note: Brown University's full accounting (2001–2021) includes the pre-2011 period not tracked here. Afghan security force and Taliban figures are estimates — no authoritative record exists.

spendChart.note: US + NATO direct war costs 2011–2021; Taliban undisclosed

parties.name: United States
parties.explainer: Direct DoD war appropriations averaged ~$42B/yr (2011–2021) per the Congressional Research Service, covering combat operations, Afghan security force training, and base infrastructure. Peak spending was ~$120B/yr in 2010–2012, declining as U.S. forces drew down from 100,000 to 2,500 troops by the 2021 withdrawal.

parties.name: NATO + Afghan Forces
parties.explainer: NATO non-US allies contributed ~$12B+ in direct war costs and training. The Afghan National Security Forces (ANSF) were funded primarily through the Afghan Security Forces Fund (~$5–6B/yr U.S. appropriation) plus Afghan domestic budget contributions. SIGAR tracked over $90B in total U.S. reconstruction aid including ANSF funding.

parties.name: Taliban
parties.explainer: Taliban funding derives from opium/heroin production (the group taxed ~90% of global heroin supply), extortion, mining, and donations from Gulf-based supporters. Estimates range from $300M to $1.5B/yr — none independently audited.

name: Nagorno-Karabakh Wars
parties: Azerbaijan (+ Turkey) vs. Armenia / Artsakh forces
sources: SIPRI; IISS; Armenian and Azerbaijani defense ministry budget data; Reuters
summary: 44-Day War (Sep-Nov 2020) followed by Azerbaijan's final offensive in Sept 2023. Azerbaijan military spending ~$2.65B/yr (SIPRI 2020); Armenia ~$630M/yr. Combined ~$3.3B/yr or ~$9M/day at peak. Blended ~$12M/day including Turkish drone operations and advisor costs. Azerbaijan seized full control Sept 19, 2023; ethnic Armenian population fled.
comparables: The 44-Day War (2020) was the first conflict to demonstrate drone warfare at decisive scale — Azerbaijan spent less on drones than a single U.S. destroyer, yet achieved strategic victory
Total conflict cost ($11B over 3 years) is less than the price of one Gerald R. Ford-class aircraft carrier ($13B) — yet permanently redrew the map of the South Caucasus
Azerbaijan's military buildup was funded almost entirely by oil revenues — it spent more on defense modernization in 5 years than Armenia's entire GDP

casualties.source: Official government figures (Azerbaijan and Armenia); ICRC; Reuters
casualties.note: Both governments published official figures for the 44-Day War. The 2023 offensive figures are less independently verified. Civilian casualties were relatively low given the intensity of combat.

spendChart.note: Azerbaijan + Turkey vs. Armenia/Artsakh; two distinct conflict phases

parties.name: Azerbaijan (+ Turkey)
parties.explainer: Azerbaijan's military budget was $2.65B/yr (SIPRI 2020), entirely funded by oil revenues, after years of buildup following the First Nagorno-Karabakh War. Turkish advisory support and Bayraktar TB2 drone systems were decisive in the 44-Day War. The 2023 offensive was brief (24 hours of decisive operations) and far less costly.

parties.name: Armenia / Artsakh
parties.explainer: Armenia's defense budget was ~$630M/yr (SIPRI 2020), less than a quarter of Azerbaijan's. Artsakh (Nagorno-Karabakh) had a separate defense force funded by Armenian diaspora and Russian equipment transfers. The 2023 offensive collapsed Artsakh's defenses in under 24 hours, ending Armenian presence in the territory.

name: War Against ISIS (Iraq/Syria)
parties: U.S.-led coalition + Iraq + Kurdish forces vs. Islamic State (ISIS/ISIL)
sources: Pentagon Cost of War reports; Congressional Research Service; Watson Institute; Defense One
summary: ISIS declared caliphate June 2014; Operation Inherent Resolve began Aug 2014. Pentagon reported ~$14M/day in U.S. costs alone at peak (2016). Full coalition including Iraq, Kurdish, French, UK operations ~$25M/day. Total U.S. cost ~$24.7B (CRS). ISIS territorial defeat at Baghouz, Syria, March 23, 2019.
comparables: ISIS at its peak self-funded on roughly $1–2M/day through oil smuggling and extortion — the coalition spent 12× that amount daily to defeat them
The U.S. alone launched 34,000+ airstrikes against ISIS — at an average cost of ~$700,000 per strike, totaling $24.7B in confirmed U.S. spending
The total coalition cost ($47B+ combined) exceeds what ISIS controlled in annual oil revenue by 20× — illustrating the profound asymmetry of modern counter-insurgency

casualties.source: Pentagon / Operation Inherent Resolve; Airwars (civilian airstrike deaths); ACLED; IBC (Iraq Body Count); CRS
casualties.note: ISIS fighter deaths are Pentagon estimates and may be overstated. Civilian deaths from coalition airstrikes are tracked by Airwars — their figures are considered more reliable than coalition self-reporting.

spendChart.note: US-led coalition vs. Iraqi/Kurdish forces; ISIS self-funded

parties.name: U.S.-Led Coalition
parties.explainer: Total confirmed U.S. cost: $24.7B (Congressional Research Service), covering 34,000+ airstrikes and Operation Inherent Resolve ground advisors. Pentagon reported ~$14M/day in U.S.-only costs at peak (2016). UK, France, Canada, and Australia added further coalition contributions, bringing the total to ~$35B.

parties.name: Iraq + Kurdish Forces (ISF/Peshmerga)
parties.explainer: Iraqi Security Forces and Kurdish Peshmerga bore the majority of ground combat against ISIS. Iraq's defense budget grew from $6B to $14B/yr during the campaign. The Kurdish Regional Government's Peshmerga received ~$800M in U.S. equipment and training. Combined ground force costs estimated at ~$9M/day average.

parties.name: ISIS / ISIL
parties.explainer: At its peak (2014–2015), ISIS self-funded at an estimated $1–2M/day through oil smuggling (~$1.5M/day), extortion, taxation, and looting — one of the most financially sophisticated terrorist organizations in history. Revenue collapsed as territory was lost.

name: Nigeria / Boko Haram + ISWAP
parties: Nigeria + MNJTF vs. Boko Haram / ISWAP
sources: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database 2020–2024 (Nigeria defense spending: $2.57B 2020, $4.47B 2021, $3.11B 2022, $3.2B 2023); ISS Africa — conflict financing analysis; Ecofin Agency (SIPRI Nigeria +56% YoY 2021); ACLED Nigeria country data; CFR Boko Haram's Deadly Impact; Euronews (Apr 13, 2026 — up to 200 civilians killed in Nigerian Air Force 'misfire' on market in Yobe state); Amnesty International (Apr 13, 2026 — at least 100 killed in airstrike on village near Borno-Yobe border); Alexa.ng (Apr 2026 — Boko Haram attack on Monguno outpost: Army Colonel and dozens of soldiers dead); Federal Ministry of Information and National Orientation (Apr 15, 2026 — government on Jilli axis airstrike of April 11); PM News Nigeria (Apr 15, 2026 — Jilli axis designated major terrorist corridor); National Accord Newspaper (Apr 2026 — FG explains Jilli airstrike, orders independent probe); New Dawn Nigeria (Apr 13, 2026 — Jilli market airstrike: military action was necessary, says Buratai); Federal High Court Abuja (Apr 2026 — 386 convictions from 508 terrorism-related cases); Global Centre for R2P Nigeria 2026; HRW World Report 2026 Nigeria; International Christian Concern (Mar 30, 2026 — Palm Sunday attacks); Critical Threats (Feb 5, 2026 — Boko massacre in Nigeria); Soufan Center (May 21, 2025 — ISWAP tactical evolution)
summary: Boko Haram's violent insurgency erupted July 2009 in Maiduguri, northeastern Nigeria. The group fractured in 2016 — Boko Haram (JAS) weakened, ISWAP (Islamic State West Africa Province) expanded. Nigeria's defense budget grew from ~$1.5B/yr in 2009 to $4.47B in 2021 (SIPRI), driven substantially by the northeast campaign. Nigeria also leads the Multinational Joint Task Force (MNJTF) with Cameroon, Chad, and Niger. Approximately 40–50% of Nigeria's defense spending — plus MNJTF contributions — is attributed to the northeast theater, averaging ~$2.5M/day over the conflict's lifetime. ISWAP now controls larger territory than Boko Haram ever did. 50,000+ killed; 2M+ IDPs in Borno, Yobe, and Adamawa states. APRIL 2026: On April 11, 2026, the Nigerian Air Force under Operation HADIN KAI conducted a strike on the Jilli axis, Gubio Local Government Area of Borno State — a known Boko Haram/ISWAP logistics hub ("Kasu Daulaye," or the terrorists' market) closed to civilians for 5 years. Government described it as an "intelligence-driven counter-terrorism mission" targeting terrorist supply lines. The Federal Government ordered a full independent investigation. On April 12, an ISWAP courier (15-year-old Tijjani) was captured in Ngamdu, confirming ongoing logistics activity in the area. Note: this is separate from the April 13 Yobe market misfire incident (up to 200 killed per Amnesty/Euronews). In other developments, 386 convictions from 508 terrorism-related cases were secured at the Federal High Court in Abuja — a landmark counter-terrorism legal milestone. JNIM has also now claimed attacks inside Nigeria's Kwara/Niger border area for the first time (cross-border Sahel spillover).
comparables: Nigeria's annual northeast security cost exceeds the entire national budget for universal basic education — for a country where 10 million children are out of school
The total estimated cost ($15B+) is more than twice Nigeria's peak annual oil revenue during the 2016 oil-price crash — spent fighting an insurgency that has lasted longer than WWII
The Lake Chad Basin humanitarian crisis (10.7M+ affected) costs the international community more to respond to each year than Nigeria spends fighting the insurgency itself

casualties.source: ACLED Nigeria (34,261 documented conflict deaths Jun 2011–Jun 2018); CFR Boko Haram's Deadly Impact (50,000+ total cited); Global Centre for R2P; ISS Africa; UNHCR; HRW World Report 2026; Critical Threats Africa File (Feb 5, 2026); International Christian Concern (Mar 30, 2026); Daily Post Nigeria (Apr 5, 2026 — Police repel Boko Haram/ISWAP attacks in Borno); The Defense Post (Apr 4, 2026 — dozens killed in Nigeria jihadist attacks on loggers and Chibok area village); Euronews (Apr 13, 2026 — Air Force market misfire, up to 200 civilians killed); Amnesty International (Apr 13, 2026 — at least 100 killed); Alexa.ng (Apr 2026 — Monguno outpost attack, Army Colonel + 22+ soldiers killed); Federal Ministry of Information and National Orientation (Apr 15, 2026 — Jilli axis airstrike); National Accord / New Dawn Nigeria (Apr 13-15, 2026 — Jilli investigation ordered); Federal High Court Abuja (Apr 2026 — 386 convictions)
casualties.note: ACLED documented 34,261 killed in 2011–2018 alone. The total since 2009 is widely cited as 50,000–60,000+. ISWAP has intensified operations significantly in 2025–26: daily attacks on civilians and security forces in Yobe and Borno states. Notable 2026 incidents include: a massacre of 160–170 civilians in two neighboring Kwara State villages on February 3, 2026 (one of the deadliest single attacks in years); the abduction of 300+ civilians in Ngoshe, Borno State in early March 2026; suspected suicide bombings in northeastern Nigeria in March 2026 killing 23 people and wounding 108; multiple coordinated bombings in Maiduguri on March 16, 2026; a Palm Sunday attack in Ungwan Rukuba, Jos North (Plateau State) on March 30, 2026, killing at least 30 people. APRIL 5, 2026: Borno State Police repelled coordinated Boko Haram/ISWAP attacks on Nganzai (attempted seizure of divisional police headquarters using RPGs) and Damasak communities; on the same day, armed terrorists attacked two churches in Kaduna's Ariko community during Easter celebrations, causing multiple fatalities. Jihadists intensified direct attacks against the Nigerian military in March 2026, killing officers and seizing weapons (Washington Post, Mar 10, 2026). Nigeria's armed forces are now deployed in two-thirds of all states — severely overstretched. APRIL 4, 2026: ISWAP killed at least 27 loggers and abducted 18 others in a forest outside Mafa town, Borno State; on the same day ISWAP fighters raided Kautikeri village outside Chibok, killing 11 people and burning homes — at least 38 killed across both attacks (The Defense Post, Apr 4, 2026). Civilian deaths remain heavily undercounted given severe access restrictions. APRIL 13, 2026 — NIGERIAN AIR FORCE MISFIRE: The Nigerian Air Force struck a local market in the northeast, killing up to 200 civilians — one of the deadliest reported incidents of accidental civilian killing in the conflict's history. The strike targeted jihadist rebels but hit a village market near the Borno-Yobe border. Amnesty International cited survivors reporting at least 100 killed; some sources said up to 200. Nigerian military misfires have killed at least 500 civilians since 2017. BOKO HARAM ATTACK ON MONGUNO (Apr 2026): Suspected Boko Haram insurgents launched a coordinated assault on a Nigerian Army outpost in Monguno, Borno State, killing at least 22 soldiers including one army colonel. APRIL 11, 2026: Nigerian Air Force struck Jilli axis (Borno State) in a deliberate operation targeting what the government designated a Boko Haram/ISWAP supply hub ("Kasu Daulaye" — terrorist market, closed 5 years). FG ordered independent investigation. Former Army Chief Buratai said the area was a known terrorist logistics site. Note: this is a separate incident from the April 13 Yobe state market strike (up to 200 civilians killed per Amnesty International/Euronews). JNIM has for the first time claimed attacks inside Nigeria (Kwara/Niger border), marking a new Sahel spillover. 386 terrorism convictions secured at Federal High Court Abuja (April 2026). APRIL 2026 UPDATE: The 2026 early-year attacks in Nigeria have been particularly severe: Kwara State Feb 3 massacre (~160 killed in two Muslim-majority villages), Ngoshe Borno abductions (~300+ civilians abducted, many killed between Mar 4-6), and an additional northwest Nigeria massacre of ~170 civilians (Jan). Palm Sunday attacks killed dozens more in late March. Nigerian armed forces are deployed in two-thirds of states and remain stretched thin.

entries.label: Lake Chad Basin — total humanitarian crisis (affected)

spendChart.note: Nigerian defense budget estimate; Boko Haram/ISWAP spending undisclosed

parties.name: Nigeria + MNJTF
parties.explainer: Nigeria's defense budget grew from ~$1.5B/yr (2009) to $4.47B (2021, SIPRI) — a 3× increase driven substantially by the northeast campaign. The Multinational Joint Task Force (MNJTF), incorporating Cameroon, Chad, and Niger, adds further regional costs. An estimated 40–50% of Nigeria's defense budget is attributable to the Boko Haram/ISWAP theater.

parties.name: Boko Haram / ISWAP
parties.explainer: Boko Haram and its ISWAP splinter are self-financing through kidnap-for-ransom, cattle rustling, taxation of Lake Chad fishing communities, and Islamic State global network support. ISWAP now controls larger territory than Boko Haram ever did, generating substantial local revenue — no reliable budget exists.

name: Somalia / Al-Shabaab War
parties: Somalia + AU Mission (AMISOM/ATMIS/AUSSOM) + U.S. vs. Al-Shabaab
sources: ISS Africa (ATMIS annual cost $1.5B in 2023); IPI — Lessons from AMISOM (2019, initial budget $622M); EU Commission (€4.3B committed since 2007); IPI Global Observatory — AUSSOM $196M 2025 budget; CFR Global Conflict Tracker; ACLED Somalia; VOA — Al-Shabaab 4,000+ civilian deaths; AFRICOM press release (Apr 9, 2026 — airstrike targeting Al-Shabaab near Qalay, 131 km NW of Kismayo); AFRICOM press release (Apr 13, 2026 — further airstrike targeting Al-Shabaab); Al Jazeera (Apr 14, 2026 — Somalia reports hit on Al-Shabaab with international support); The Human Rights / Somalia (Apr 2026 — Al-Shabaab offensive in southern regions); AUSSOM (2025-2026 budget: $166.5M based on $828/troop reimbursement rate per UN SG report); UN Security Council (May 12, 2025 — failed to activate UNSC Res. 2719 for AUSSOM funding); The Human Rights Weekly (2026 — Al-Shabaab offensive 2026 update); Defcon Level (2026 — Somalia conflict & Al-Shabaab threat); Crisis Group (Apr 2026 — sustaining gains in Somalia's offensive); CTC West Point (Apr 2026 — Somalia at a crossroads); Anadolu Agency (Apr 2026 — heavy casualties Ethiopian peacekeepers)
summary: AMISOM deployed March 2007 to support Somalia's Transitional Federal Government. Mission peaked at 22,000 troops, grew from $350M/yr to ~$1B/yr (AMISOM), then $1.5B/yr (ATMIS, 2022–2023). The EU has invested €4.3B ($4.7B) in Somalia security since 2007; U.S. spent ~$2B in counter-terrorism assistance 2007–2020 with ongoing airstrikes. Al-Shabaab controls significant rural territory despite 18 years of operations. Current mission (AUSSOM, 2025) budgeted at just $196M — a severe reduction that threatens the mission's viability. Al-Shabaab remains the most prolific Al-Qaeda affiliate by attack volume. $2M/day reflects a mission-lifetime average across escalating and de-escalating phases. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: AFRICOM conducted airstrikes targeting Al-Shabaab on April 9 (near Qalay, ~131 km NW of Kismayo) and April 13, 2026, in coordination with the Federal Government of Somalia. Al-Shabaab has retaken at least 5 districts in Lower Shabelle and Middle Jubba since late 2025 as AUSSOM contracted. A February 2026 bombing near Mogadishu International Airport killed 30+ people. AUSSOM funding: the UN SG report confirms the July 2025–June 2026 budget at $166.5M (based on $828/troop reimbursement) — sharply below the prior $1.5B/yr ATMIS level. Current AUSSOM strength: ~11,146 uniformed personnel. On May 12, 2025, the UN Security Council failed to activate UNSC Resolution 2719 for AUSSOM funding (due to lack of U.S. support), casting serious doubt over mission viability. Al-Shabaab is AFRICOM's designated "largest, wealthiest, and most lethal" Al-Qaeda affiliate globally. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: Al-Shabaab has exploited the AUSSOM funding collapse and retaken at least five districts in Lower Shabelle and Middle Jubba regions. Major recent attacks: February 2026 suicide bombing near Mogadishu International Airport killed 30+; March 11 Beledweyne hotel siege killed 10 (civilians + military); March 18 attempt on President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud's motorcade killed 4 (president unharmed). AUSSOM is re-authorized to Dec 31, 2026 with up to 11,826 personnel but the funding gap vs ATMIS's $1.5B/yr remains acute.
comparables: The EU alone has invested €4.3 billion in Somalia's security since 2007 — enough to reconstruct the entire country's road network three times over
Al-Shabaab remains the world's most active Al-Qaeda affiliate despite 18 years of AU peacekeeping and $16B+ in external security spending — illustrating the limits of military solutions to insurgency
AMISOM at peak deployed 22,000 troops — more soldiers than the entire standing armies of 70 UN member states — yet could not permanently dislodge Al-Shabaab from rural Somalia

casualties.source: ACLED Somalia (4,000+ civilian deaths from Al-Shabaab attacks, 2010–2021, per VOA); ACLED 2024 Al-Shabaab activity mapping; Mogadishu Medina Hospital data (54,000+ injuries since 2007); CFR Al-Shabaab conflict tracker; UNHCR; CTC West Point — Somalia at a Crossroads (2026); UN Security Council Resolution 2809 (2025); AU Peace and Security Council communiqué Feb 12, 2026; AFRICOM (Apr 9, 2026 — airstrike near Qalay); AFRICOM (Apr 13, 2026 — additional airstrike); Al Jazeera (Apr 14, 2026 — Somalia reports Al-Shabaab hit); Human Rights Monitor Somalia (Apr 2026 — Al-Shabaab southern offensive); AUSSOM UN SG Report (2025-2026 — $166.5M budget, 11,146 troops)
casualties.note: ACLED's civilian-targeting figures (4,000+ since 2010) are a floor. The AUSSOM mission was re-authorized by the UN Security Council to December 31, 2026, with up to 11,826 uniformed personnel. The severe funding reduction from ATMIS ($1.5B/yr) to AUSSOM ($196M budget) threatens mission viability. Al-Shabaab lost its leader in a U.S.-enabled kill announced in February 2026 but the group remains resilient — AUSSOM has denied Al-Shabaab's competing casualty claims. Somali forces and AU troops killed 40+ Al-Shabaab militants in a single Lower Shabelle engagement at Mubarak (March 2026, Axadle/AUSSOM). On April 2, 2026, a joint AUSSOM-SNAF operation captured senior Al-Shabaab battalion commander Salaad Cusmaan Macalin ('Sahm'), accompanied by 17 foreign fighters from Al-Shabaab's Jilib command center — one of the most significant commander captures in recent months. 30 militants were killed in a January 2026 joint operation in Lower Shabelle. AUSSOM was reduced from 22,000+ to roughly 12,000 troops in 2025. 2022 was the deadliest year since 2018, with 6,500+ fatalities (ACLED). APRIL 2026 UPDATE: AFRICOM conducted airstrikes targeting Al-Shabaab on April 9 (Qalay area) and April 13, 2026. Al-Shabaab retook at least 5 districts in Lower Shabelle and Middle Jubba by early 2026 as AUSSOM drew down. A February 2026 bombing near Mogadishu airport killed 30+. AUSSOM confirmed at ~11,146 troops; July 2025-June 2026 budget at $166.5M. UN Security Council (May 12, 2025) failed to activate Resolution 2719 for AUSSOM funding. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: Al-Shabaab has been resurgent through early 2026 — retaking districts in Lower Shabelle and Middle Jubba, and conducting high-profile attacks in Mogadishu (Feb airport bombing 30+ killed; Mar 11 Beledweyne hotel attack 10 killed; Mar 18 presidential motorcade attack 4 killed).

spendChart.note: AMISOM/ATMIS/AUSSOM mission lifetime average; Al-Shabaab undisclosed

parties.name: Somalia + AU Mission (AMISOM → AUSSOM)
parties.explainer: AMISOM peaked at 22,000 troops with a budget of ~$1B/yr, funded primarily by the EU (€4.3B committed since 2007) and U.S. counter-terrorism assistance (~$2B over 2007–2020). Mission transitioned to ATMIS ($1.5B/yr, 2022–2023) then dramatically contracted to AUSSOM ($166.5M confirmed July 2025-June 2026 budget per UN SG report; $196M originally budgeted) — an 87% funding cut that severely threatens operational capacity.

parties.name: Al-Shabaab
parties.explainer: Al-Shabaab self-finances through port taxation at Kismayo and other towns, extortion of businesses and NGOs, and international Al-Qaeda network transfers. The UN Monitoring Group estimated revenues of $50–100M/yr — making it one of the wealthiest non-state armed groups in Africa. No auditable budget exists.

name: South Sudan Civil War
parties: SPLA / government forces (Kiir) + Uganda vs. SPLM-IO (Machar) + UNMISS (UN peacekeeping)
sources: SIPRI (South Sudan military 2012: $612M / 8.4% GDP); CFR Global Conflict Tracker — South Sudan; CRS / FAS IN10975 (nearly 400,000 estimated dead); Watson Institute Costs of War; UNMISS budget data (~$1B/yr UN peacekeeping); UNHCR; OCHA
summary: Civil war erupted December 15, 2013 after a political rupture between President Salva Kiir and Vice President Riek Machar. Uganda deployed forces in support of Kiir 2013–2015 (estimated $600–800M/yr). UNMISS expanded to 17,000 troops with UN budget of ~$1B/yr throughout the conflict. South Sudan's own military spending ranged from $400M–$800M/yr. Combined direct military cost estimated at $3.5M/day average. Transitional Government of National Unity formed February 22, 2020, ending the primary conflict phase. Approximately 400,000 excess deaths; 4M+ IDPs; 2.5M+ refugees.
comparables: The war killed approximately 400,000 people — equivalent to the entire population of New Orleans — in a country of just 11 million, making this one of the deadliest post-Cold War conflicts
UNMISS alone cost the UN ~$1B/year — more than all infrastructure investment in South Sudan since its independence in 2011
In 2016, the UN estimated 65% of South Sudan's population needed emergency food aid — while both warring factions spent millions per day on arms and soldiers

casualties.source: Harvard School of Public Health / London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine (2018 peer-reviewed excess death estimate: 383,000); CRS IN10975 (citing ~400,000); UNHCR; OCHA; Amnesty International
casualties.note: The ~383,000 figure (Harvard/LSHTM, 2018 peer-reviewed) is an excess death estimate through mid-2018 covering all causes — combat, disease, and starvation. Total deaths through the 2020 peace deal are almost certainly higher. Direct combat deaths are estimated at roughly half the total; the remainder from famine and disease caused by the conflict. Access restrictions severely limited independent verification throughout.

spendChart.note: Combined SPLA, SPLM-IO, Uganda, and UNMISS peacekeeping costs

parties.name: SPLA / Government + Uganda
parties.explainer: South Sudan's military spending peaked at $800M/yr (SIPRI), consuming 60%+ of government revenue during the conflict's worst phases. Uganda deployed forces in support of President Kiir (2013–2015), estimated at $600–800M total. UNMISS (UN peacekeeping) operated at ~$1B/yr — the largest single cost component — separately funded by UN member states.

parties.name: SPLM-IO (Machar) + Allies
parties.explainer: Riek Machar's SPLM-IO was supported by South Sudanese diaspora, captured government equipment, and reportedly Sudan's intelligence services. The group had no formal budget — funding came through looting, cattle raiding, and external patronage. No independent cost figure exists.

name: Mozambique / Cabo Delgado
parties: Mozambique + Rwanda vs. Ansar al-Sunna (ISIS-Mozambique / al-Shabaab)
sources: ACLED / Club of Mozambique (6,515 deaths Mar 2026); ISS Africa — SAMIM withdrawal analysis (May 2024); ISS Africa — Rwanda withdrawal threat analysis (Apr 2026); EU Council European Peace Facility (€20M for RDF, Nov 2024 — top-up); Irish Times (Mar 19, 2026 — EU pulls Rwanda funding); DefenceWeb (Apr 2026 — lessons from withdrawal threat); Rwanda government statement (Apr 3, 2026 — 6,300 troops, 'solidarity not money'); fundsforNGOs (Apr 3, 2026 — withdrawal scenario analysis); US sanctions on Rwanda Defence Force (Mar 2, 2026); TotalEnergies LNG resumption (Jan 2026); Zitamar Conflict Monitor (Mar 23–Apr 5, 2026 — 3 events, 3 fatalities); ACLED Conflict Monitor Update (Apr 8, 2026); Plataformamedia.com (Mar 17, 2026 — EU to define new security support by May); Rwanda President Kagame / Jeune Afrique (Apr 3, 2026 — oil companies should pay for security); Mozambique Cabinet (Apr 8, 2026 — new counter-terrorism strategy promised); ACLED Mozambique Conflict Monitor (Apr 8, 2026 — ISM activity in Macomia and Muidumbe); Zitamar Mozambique Conflict Monitor (Mar 23–Apr 5, 2026); African Security Analysis (Apr 2026 — Mozambique renews Rwandan military partnership as Cabo Delgado insurgency escalates); UN OCHA (2026 — appealed for $348M for 1.1M affected); ACLED Cabo Ligado Monitor (Apr 2026); ISS Africa (Apr 2026 — Cabo Delgado insurgency persists amid failed military strategy); ADF Magazine (Oct 2025 — Cabo Delgado insurgency persists); The Workers' Rights (Apr 2026 — Cabo Delgado fishermen killings 2026 crisis)
summary: ISIS-linked insurgency (Ansar al-Sunna, locally called al-Shabaab) erupted October 5, 2017 in Mocímboa da Praia, Cabo Delgado. SADC deployed SAMIM in June 2021 (~$10M/month); Rwanda deployed from July 2021, now confirmed at 6,300+ troops (3× original deployment). SAMIM withdrew July 2024. EU European Peace Facility committed ~€46M total 2021–2025 — ~17% of Rwanda's costs. In March 2026, Rwanda threatened withdrawal if adequate funding not secured. The EU announced it will end Rwanda funding in May 2026. On April 3, Rwanda's President Kagame challenged oil companies (TotalEnergies, ExxonMobil) and the Mozambican government to pay ~$100M/yr for Rwanda's security provision ('figure out how to pay for the security they need'), while simultaneously affirming Rwanda was present 'for solidarity, not money' — tempering but not eliminating the withdrawal threat. RDF troops assured local communities no immediate pullout is planned. The US imposed sanctions on the Rwanda Defence Force on March 2, 2026, complicating Rwanda's international standing. The EU indicated it may define a new security support mechanism by May. SECURITY STATUS: March 23–April 5, 2026 was a relatively quiet period (3 political violence events, 3 fatalities, per Zitamar/ACLED); ISM maintained presence in Macomia and visited mine sites for suspected extortion. Mozambique's Cabinet promised a new parliamentary counter-terrorism strategy (Apr 8, 2026). TotalEnergies resumed work on its $20B LNG project (Jan 2026), which Rwanda's presence helped enable. Insurgent strength estimated at 300–1,000 fighters. $600K/day reflects conflict-lifetime average; current operational rate with 6,300+ Rwandan troops is substantially higher. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: ACLED/Cabo Ligado: 6,257 killed since the 2017 start of the insurgency, 2,631+ civilians confirmed. A FADM (Mozambican navy) patrol fired on six fishing boats off Mocímboa da Praia on March 15, killing 13 fishermen — part of a pattern in which navy patrols enforcing 'no-sail' zones have killed 85+ fishermen in 2026 alone. ISM kidnapped four fishermen in Mocímboa da Praia on March 25. Rwanda's withdrawal threat and end of EU Peace Facility funding (May 2026) remain the most consequential near-term risks.
comparables: Cabo Delgado sits above one of the world's largest natural gas reserves — TotalEnergies' $20B LNG project remains suspended, costing far more in lost investment than the entire war
Rwanda has committed hundreds of millions and thousands of troops to stabilize a neighbor's insurgency — while relying on EU support that has now been terminated with no replacement funding
The 6,500+ documented deaths make this one of Africa's least-covered active conflicts — despite the same region hosting a Western LNG investment larger than the entire Mozambican GDP

casualties.source: ACLED / Club of Mozambique (6,257 deaths — 8-year total to Oct 2025); ACLED Mozambique Conflict Monitor (6,515 total — Mar 2026); UNHCR; OCHA; Africanews (Mar 15, 2026 — Rwanda withdrawal threat); Zitamar Conflict Monitor (Mar 9–22, 2026); ACLED Conflict Monitor Update Mar 25, 2026; ACLED Mozambique Conflict Monitor Update (Apr 8, 2026); Zitamar Mozambique Conflict Monitor (Mar 23–Apr 5, 2026)
casualties.note: Of the 6,515 ACLED-documented deaths, at least 2,631 were civilians. Rwanda has 6,300+ troops deployed — confirmed by President Kagame on April 3, 2026. Rwanda threatened withdrawal in March 2026 after the EU confirmed it will end European Peace Facility funding in May 2026, with no replacement secured. Rwanda's Kagame also challenged TotalEnergies, ExxonMobil, and Mozambique to fund Rwanda's ~$100M/yr security costs directly. Rwanda subsequently clarified it is present 'for solidarity, not money' (spokesman Makolo, Apr 3, 2026), and RDF troops assured local communities no immediate withdrawal is planned. On March 2, 2026, the US imposed sanctions on the Rwanda Defence Force and senior officials. Mozambique's Cabinet promised Parliament a new counter-terrorism strategy (Apr 8, 2026). March 23–April 5 was a relatively quiet period: only 3 political violence events and 3 fatalities per Zitamar/ACLED — the lowest tempo in months. ISM maintained presence in Macomia and conducted suspected extortion visits to mine sites in Meluco. TotalEnergies resumed its $20B LNG project in January 2026 — which Rwanda's presence helped enable. The March 15 FADM killing of 13 civilians on fishing boats near Calugo drew condemnation. Insurgent membership estimated at 300–1,000 fighters. MARCH–APRIL 2026: On March 31, a group of up to 30 militants attacked fishers at Nguri Lake in Muidumbe district (coming by boat along the Messalo River). On March 30-31, a large group of over 100 insurgents visited gold mine sites at Minhanha and Ravia in Macomia district, extorting artisanal miners. ACLED (Apr 8, 2026) confirms ISM maintains a presence in Macomia and Muidumbe despite recent Mozambican-Rwandan operations in Catupa forest. Rwanda may withdraw its forces unless oil companies (TotalEnergies, ExxonMobil) and the Mozambican government pay Rwanda ~$100M/year for security costs (Kagame statement). UN OCHA appealed for $348M in 2026 for 1.1M conflict-affected persons — secured only ~one-third of required funding. APRIL 16, 2026 STATUS: The situation remains in a funding-crisis holding pattern — Rwanda has not withdrawn despite threats, the EU is ending its EPF contribution in May, and negotiations with TotalEnergies/ExxonMobil/Mozambique on long-term security funding are unresolved. Security tempo remains low (3 events, 3 fatalities in March 23-April 5 period). A group of 4 former ISM members (3 men, 1 woman) appeared in Mucojo area on April 29 seeking to surrender — Rwandan forces received them. ISM continues extortion of artisanal miners in Macomia and Muidumbe. The FADM navy killed at least 13 fishers on March 15 near Mocímboa da Praia — continuing a pattern of FADM attacks on civilian boats that risks building resentment toward the state. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: ACLED/Cabo Ligado total is 6,257 (2,631+ civilians) as of the April 2026 monitor release. 85+ fishermen killed in 2026 alone by FADM naval patrols enforcing no-sail zones.

parties.name: Ansar al-Sunna (ISIS-Mozambique)
parties.explainer: The insurgency (locally called al-Shabaab, distinct from Somalia) is estimated at 300–1,000 active fighters. Funding derives from local taxation, gem and timber smuggling from Cabo Delgado's rich natural resources, and Islamic State network connections. No independent budget figure exists.

name: Pakistan–Afghanistan War 2026
parties: Pakistan vs. Afghan Taliban (Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan)
sources: Military.com (Feb 27, 2026 — Pakistan declares open war, airstrikes on Kabul and Kandahar); Geo.tv Operation Ghazab lil-Haq live updates; War on the Rocks (Mar 2026 — Can Pakistan's escalation compel a Taliban recalculation?); Pakistan Today (Mar 26, 2026 — Operation resumes, Torkham border reopens); Times of Islamabad (Mar 25, 2026 — Pakistan shifts battlefield strategy); Wikipedia — 2026 Afghanistan–Pakistan conflict; Al Jazeera (Mar 16, 2026 — Afghanistan accuses Pakistan of killing 400 in attack on Kabul hospital); NPR (Mar 18, 2026 — at least 400 dead in drug rehabilitation center strike in Kabul); UNAMA (Mar 16-18, 2026 — at least 143 killed confirmed at Omid Hospital); HRW (Mar 27, 2026 — Pakistan airstrike on Afghan medical facility unlawful); Washington Post / AP (Apr 1, 2026 — China hosts Pakistan-Afghanistan ceasefire talks in Urumqi); NPR (Apr 3, 2026 — Pakistan says new round of peace talks underway in China); UN OCHA Afghanistan Situation Update #3 (Apr 5, 2026 — 94,000+ displaced); Washington Post (Apr 8, 2026 — Afghanistan and Pakistan agree to ease tensions after China-hosted Urumqi talks); China MFA / Mao Ning (Apr 7, 2026 — all parties acknowledged terrorism is core issue); Dropsite News (Apr 9-10, 2026 — China brokers deal in Afghanistan-Pakistan war); Washington Times (Apr 14, 2026 — Pakistan's quiet military success); BAMF Briefing Notes CW16/2026 (Apr 2026 — Pakistan-Afghanistan conflict update); Wikipedia 2026 Afghanistan-Pakistan war (Apr 2026); OHCHR (Mar 2026 — Türk says Pakistan-Afghanistan conflict piles misery on misery); BAMF briefing notes CW 16/2026; Jurist (Mar 2026 — Pakistan declares open war against Afghanistan); OHCHR (Mar 2026 — Afghan-Pakistani border UN experts urgently call for lasting peace); OCHA Afghanistan Situation Update #3 (Apr 5, 2026)
summary: On February 27, 2026, Pakistan launched Operation Ghazab lil-Haq ('Wrath for Justice') — a large-scale air and ground campaign against Afghan Taliban positions across multiple provinces including Kabul, Kandahar, Paktia, Nangarhar, Khost, and Paktika. Pakistan cited the Taliban's sheltering of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP), which had carried out attacks inside Pakistan. Pakistan struck 22+ locations and claims 51 total target sites hit in airstrikes. Pakistan secured 32 sq km of strategic Afghan territory (Ghudwana enclave) in the Zhob sector. The operation continued into late March with resumed strikes after an Eid ceasefire (Mar 18-24). KABUL HOSPITAL STRIKE (Mar 16, 2026): Pakistan Air Force strikes hit the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital (drug rehabilitation center) in Kabul — the deadliest single incident of the conflict. Afghan Taliban claimed 408 killed and 265 wounded; UNAMA independently confirmed at least 143 killed and 119+ injured in an ongoing investigation. Human Rights Watch ruled the strike 'unlawful' on March 27, 2026. Pakistan denied directly targeting the hospital, claiming its forces struck nearby military sites. CHINA-BROKERED TALKS: Pakistan and Afghanistan held seven days of talks mediated by China in Urumqi (April 1-7, 2026). China announced both sides agreed to 'refrain from actions that may escalate or complicate the situation' and to 'explore a comprehensive solution'; Afghanistan's Taliban branded the talks 'useful.' No formal ceasefire was agreed — hostilities continue as of April 11, 2026. Pakistan simultaneously brokered the April 8 U.S.-Iran ceasefire (Islamabad Accords), demonstrating significant diplomatic reach. Pakistan's $10B/yr defense budget (~$27M/day total) supports a major multi-front air and ground campaign; $5M/day is a conservative operational cost estimate, though reliable independent cost data is not yet available. Pakistan simultaneously hosted the failed Islamabad talks (April 10–12) in its capacity as mediator between the U.S. and Iran — a ceasefire that itself now faces uncertainty following the Islamabad collapse. APRIL 14, 2026: The Washington Times assessed Pakistan's Operation Ghazab lil-Haq as achieving 'quiet military success' — precision strikes targeting TTP sanctuaries while attempting to minimise civilian casualties. Chinese-mediated back-channel contact continues. Pakistan's simultaneous role as mediator in the collapsed U.S.-Iran Islamabad talks (Apr 10-12) underscores its diplomatic centrality in the region, though the collapse has added pressure to its foreign policy posture. Operations ongoing at reduced tempo as of April 14. URUMQI TALKS CONCLUSION: China-mediated talks in Urumqi formally concluded April 8 without a formal ceasefire. General Munir stated: "Ghazab-Lil-Haq will continue until TTP safe havens are dismantled." Both sides agreed on non-escalation but no substantive terms were reached. BAMF Briefing Notes CW16/2026 (April 14-20, 2026) confirms the conflict remains active with operations continuing at reduced tempo. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: China-mediated peace talks held in Ürümqi (Xinjiang) ended April 8, 2026 without resolution. Pakistani COAS General Asim Munir reaffirmed Operation Ghazab-Lil-Haq will continue 'until TTP safe havens in Afghanistan are eliminated.' Afghan Taliban border forces withdrew from several posts in Nuristan (Barg-e Matal, Kamdesh) and Kunar (Nari district) after April 10 Pakistani attacks. Two civilians killed in Kunar on April 9 during fighting. Total Afghan civilian casualties since Feb 26: 76 killed, 213 injured (UN figures); Pakistani claim: 796 Afghan Taliban/TTP killed, 1,043 wounded. 115,000+ Afghans displaced.
comparables: Pakistan is fighting on two fronts simultaneously — the Taliban across the Durand Line, and TTP insurgents inside its own borders — with a military budget smaller than what India spends in 3 weeks
The Durand Line — the disputed border at the heart of this conflict — was drawn by a British colonial administrator in 1893; Afghanistan has never formally recognised it
Pakistan struck Kabul and Kandahar — cities of 4M+ and 600,000 people respectively — while itself hosting one of the world's largest Afghan refugee populations (1.3M+ registered)

casualties.source: Pakistani military statements (Mar–Apr 2026 — 435+ Taliban killed, 630+ wounded claimed); Afghan Taliban statements (disputed); Military.com (Feb 27, 2026); Pakistan Today (Mar 26, 2026); UN OCHA Afghanistan Situation Updates #2 and #3 (Apr 5, 2026); Al Jazeera (Feb 27, 2026 — contradictory death tolls); OHCHR (Mar 2026 — piles misery on misery); Kabul Tribune (Apr 2026 — 94,000+ displaced); Al Jazeera (Mar 16, 2026 — Afghanistan accuses Pakistan of killing 400 in Kabul hospital attack); UNAMA (Mar 16-18, 2026 — Omid Hospital: 143+ killed confirmed); NPR (Mar 18, 2026 — 400 dead in drug rehabilitation center strike); HRW (Mar 27, 2026 — airstrike on Afghan medical facility ruled unlawful)
casualties.note: Both sides release sharply contradictory figures. OCHA Situation Update #3 (Apr 5, 2026): over 94,000 people (13,400+ families) displaced across Khost (26,200), Kunar (25,300), Nangarhar (19,600), Paktia (18,200), and Paktika (4,900). KABUL HOSPITAL STRIKE (Mar 16, 2026): Pakistan Air Force struck the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital — the war's deadliest single incident. Afghan Taliban claim 408 killed and 265 wounded; UNAMA independently confirmed at least 143 killed and 119+ injured (ongoing investigation); HRW ruled the strike unlawful (Mar 27). Pakistan denied directly targeting the facility. Taliban cumulative civilian casualty claim (Feb 22 – Apr 3): 761 killed, 626 injured, ~15,000 mortar rounds and rockets fired by Pakistan. Pakistani official claim: 435+ Taliban/fighters killed, 630+ wounded as of late March. Afghan Defence Ministry estimated ~150 Pakistani troops killed — contested by Pakistan. A temporary Eid ceasefire (Mar 18/19 – Mar 23/24) was observed; operations resumed after. Urumqi peace talks (Apr 1-7, 2026): non-escalation agreement reached, no formal ceasefire. Operations continue at reduced tempo as of April 11. APRIL 2026 UPDATE: UN civilian casualty count since Feb 26 stands at 76 killed, 213 wounded. Pakistani military claims: 796 Afghan Taliban/TTP killed, 1,043 wounded. 115,000+ Afghans displaced. China-mediated Ürümqi talks ended Apr 8 without resolution.
