name: Venezuela / Operation Absolute Resolve
parties: United States vs. Venezuela (Maduro government / FANB loyalists)
sources: CSIS (Feb 2026 — Cancian: $31M/day ongoing operations around Venezuela, $2.8M/day unbudgeted above baseline); Bloomberg (Feb 2026 — $3B spent mid-Nov 2025 to mid-Jan 2026; $20M/day peak for naval presence); Fortune (Jan 2026 — naval blockade $700M and rising at $9M/day); CNBC (Jan 7, 2026 — 100+ killed per Venezuelan Interior Minister); Washington Post (Jan 6, 2026 — ~75 killed, U.S. officials assess); NPR (Mar 2026 — ongoing); CFR Global Conflict Tracker — U.S. Confrontation with Venezuela; Al Jazeera (Jan 2026 — remote coercion); Airwars (Jan–Mar 2026 — civilian harm documentation); The Intercept (Jan 13, 2026 — SOUTHCOM civilian harm team gutted); Wikipedia — 2026 United States intervention in Venezuela; WOLA (Jan 2026 — international law analysis); GlobalSecurity.org — Operation Absolute Resolve; AS/COA (Jan 2026 — timeline of U.S. military ramp-up); Britannica (Apr 2026 — Operation Absolute Resolve update); Everbridge (2026 — Venezuela in flux after Operation Absolute Resolve)
summary: On January 3, 2026, the United States launched Operation Absolute Resolve — a Delta Force-led decapitation strike on Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro's compound in Caracas. Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores were captured and transported to New York City to face drug trafficking and terrorism charges; both pleaded not guilty in Manhattan federal court on January 5. The operation was preceded by Operation Southern Spear — a months-long Caribbean military buildup beginning ~August 2025, with a naval blockade of sanctioned oil tankers starting December 2025. Fortune reported the naval blockade cost $700M by early January, rising at $9M/day. Bloomberg estimated total costs from mid-November 2025 through mid-January 2026 at approximately $3 billion (~$20M/day peak for the naval presence; the January 3 operation spiked costs significantly higher). Following the raid, Vice President Delcy Rodríguez was sworn in as acting president. The U.S. maintains an ongoing Caribbean naval presence (an estimated 15,000–20,000 troops in the region, including USS Ford CSG and USS Iwo Jima ARG) and continues the oil-tanker blockade. CSIS estimated ongoing operations at $31M/day as of February 2026, with ~$2.8M/day above-baseline unbudgeted costs. Under U.S. direction, Venezuela's oil sales are managed through an interim arrangement: the Rodríguez administration submits a monthly budget to Washington covering proceeds from U.S.-directed oil sales. The Trump administration has not ruled out future ground operations. The U.S. legal justification was disputed by the UN Charter and international law experts; multiple UN Security Council resolutions addressing the intervention were tabled. Anchor of $1.5B reflects the excess spending above the flat $31M/day rate during the high-intensity November–January buildup and operation. APRIL 2026 STATUS: Venezuela remains under interim President Delcy Rodríguez (sworn in January 5, 2026). Maduro and his wife remain in U.S. custody (pleaded not guilty in Manhattan federal court on drug trafficking and narco-terrorism charges). The U.S. Caribbean naval presence — estimated 15,000-20,000 troops including USS Ford CSG and USS Iwo Jima ARG — continues. The oil blockade and revenue-sharing arrangement remain in effect, with Venezuela's oil proceeds managed under U.S. direction. Trump stated the U.S. will "run it essentially until such time as a proper transition can take place." The IMF has flagged economic disruption from the Venezuela situation as a contributor to global instability. No major new military engagements reported in April 2026.
comparables: Operation Absolute Resolve spent ~$3B in its first two months — more than the total U.S. military aid to Colombia under Plan Colombia ($10B over 16 years) in under 60 days
At $31M/day for the ongoing naval presence, the U.S. Caribbean operation costs more annually ($11B+) than Venezuela's entire pre-crisis defense budget
The U.S. deployed an estimated 15,000–20,000 troops to the Caribbean — more than the combined standing armies of 30 UN member states — to capture one head of state

casualties.source: Washington Post (Jan 6, 2026 — ~75 killed, U.S. officials assess); CNBC (Jan 7, 2026 — 100+ killed, Venezuelan Interior Minister Cabello); Cuba government statement (Jan 2026 — 32 Cuban military/intelligence killed); Airwars (Jan–Mar 2026 — 7 civilian harm incidents documented); The Intercept (Jan 13, 2026 — SOUTHCOM civilian harm assessment team gutted before operation); Everbridge crisis analysis (Jan 2026)
casualties.note: Casualty figures for Operation Absolute Resolve (January 3, 2026) are disputed and incomplete. U.S. officials assessed approximately 75 killed (Washington Post, Jan 6); Venezuela's Interior Minister Diosdado Cabello claimed 100+ killed; Cuba stated 32 of its military and intelligence personnel were killed during the operation. Seven U.S. service members were injured; no U.S. KIA confirmed. Airwars documented reports of civilian harm in at least 7 incidents, noting that SOUTHCOM gutted its civilian harm assessment capacity before the operation — The Intercept reported this capacity had been 'gutted' in the months prior. The Venezuelan military largely did not resist the U.S. operation at scale. The ongoing naval blockade and sustained Caribbean military presence have not generated confirmed direct casualty reports from 2026 operations, but the Trump administration has confirmed continuing strikes against alleged drug-trafficking vessels in the Caribbean. Data quality is limited by U.S. classification and Venezuelan state restrictions on independent verification.

Fortune noted the naval blockade alone cost $700M by early January, rising at $9M/day. parties.name: Venezuela (FANB + PSUV loyalists)
parties.explainer: Venezuela's Fuerza Armada Nacional Bolivariana (FANB) largely did not resist Operation Absolute Resolve at scale. The FANB defense budget (~$1.7B/yr) is not publicly audited. Significant Russian and Chinese military equipment was destroyed or neutralized during the operation. Post-Maduro loyalist activity and any associated costs are not independently quantifiable.
